proposals.title: Mechanisms of Microaggressions in Clinical Encounters: A Qualitative Study of Patient-Perceived Discriminatory Medical Service (DMS) in Community Health Settings
proposals.motivation: Prior quantitative work establishes a strong association between perceived discrimination in healthcare settings (Discriminatory Medical Service, or DMS) and adverse mental and physical health outcomes, particularly among racial/ethnic minority youth. However, the specific, observable provider behaviors and clinical interaction contexts that patients identify as discriminatory remain poorly characterized, limiting the development of effective, targeted interventions to improve care equity.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients from minoritized backgrounds can articulate specific, recurring patterns of verbal and non-verbal provider behavior, as well as clinical environmental cues, that they interpret as discriminatory, and these mechanisms vary systematically by clinical context (e.g., mental health vs. primary care visits).
proposals.method: We will conduct semi-structured, in-depth interviews with 30-40 adolescents and young adults of African and Latinx descent (ages 16-24) recruited from a community health system, who have previously reported high levels of DMS on validated surveys. Using a phenomenological approach, interviews will focus on eliciting detailed narratives of specific healthcare encounters. Data will be analyzed via a multi-stage thematic analysis, beginning with open coding, followed by axial coding to identify core categories of behavior (e.g., dismissive communication, rushed interactions, assumptions based on race) and context, using NVivo 12 software for data management. Analysis will employ constant comparative methods to refine themes until saturation is achieved.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate a fine-grained, behaviorally-anchored taxonomy of DMS mechanisms from the patient perspective, moving beyond broad associations to actionable descriptions. It will directly inform the refinement of provider training modules and clinical environment audits, and provide critical qualitative depth to complement ongoing digital phenotyping work on health outcomes in these populations.
proposals.novelty: While structural racism's impact on health is well-reviewed, this study uniquely focuses on the micro-interactional, patient-defined mechanisms within clinical encounters, bridging the gap between macro-level structural analyses and individual health outcome data. It applies a rigorous qualitative lens to a phenomenon typically measured only quantitatively.

proposals.title: A Mixed-Methods, Community-Engaged Intervention to Reduce Discrimination in Medical Settings (DMS) for Youth of African Descent: A Pilot Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence consistently shows that discrimination in medical settings (DMS) is a pervasive stressor, contributing to health disparities and eroding trust in healthcare systems, particularly among minoritized youth. Structural racism embedded in clinical protocols, provider biases, and institutional cultures drives these harmful experiences, leading to adverse mental and physical health outcomes. Developing and testing system-level interventions is therefore a critical public health priority to improve care equity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-component intervention combining (a) an implicit bias and structural competency training module for healthcare providers, and (b) a youth- and caregiver-informed digital tool for reporting and navigating care concerns, will significantly reduce self-reported experiences of DMS and improve perceived care quality among youth of African descent in a community health setting.
proposals.method: We will employ a community-based participatory research (CBPR) framework to co-design the intervention with youth, caregivers, and providers. The provider training will be developed using established principles from the implicit association test (IAT) literature and structural competency frameworks, delivered via a series of interactive workshops. The patient-facing component will be a refined version of a digital phenotyping app, adapted from Williams' prior work, to log healthcare interactions and provide real-time support resources. We will test efficacy using a pilot stepped-wedge cluster randomized controlled trial design across several community health clinics, measuring primary outcomes via validated DMS scales and app-derived behavioral data.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel, dual-pronged intervention model that targets both provider behavior and patient empowerment within real-world community health systems. It will generate preliminary efficacy data on a scalable approach to reducing DMS and produce a co-designed digital tool that can be disseminated for further testing. The findings will directly inform health system policies and training programs aimed at mitigating the effects of structural racism in clinical care.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has documented DMS and tested standalone bias training, this proposal innovatively integrates a structural competency lens with a youth-engaged digital feedback and navigation system, creating a synergistic intervention that addresses the problem from both systemic and experiential angles within a vulnerable developmental period.

proposals.title: Intersectional Discrimination in Medical Settings (DMS): A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Unique Risk Profiles and Mental Health Outcomes Among Racially Minoritized Youth with Disabilities
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research establishes that structural racism and discrimination in medical settings (DMS) are critical social determinants of health, particularly for youth. However, studies often examine single-axis identities, obscuring the compounded experiences of individuals with multiple marginalized statuses. This gap limits our understanding of the most severe inequities and hinders the development of targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that youth at the intersection of racial/ethnic minoritization and disability status will report qualitatively distinct patterns and higher frequencies of DMS compared to their peers with only one of these identities, and that these intersectional DMS experiences will show a stronger association with adverse mental health outcomes.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent mixed-methods design. Quantitatively, we will recruit a longitudinal cohort (N=500) of racially minoritized youth (ages 14-21) from community health systems, oversampling those with disabilities. Using validated scales for DMS, depression, and anxiety, we will conduct regression analyses with interaction terms to test for effect modification by disability status. Qualitatively, we will conduct in-depth interviews (n=30-40) with a purposive subsample, using thematic analysis guided by an intersectionality framework to characterize the unique mechanisms and narratives of DMS.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first empirical models of how intersecting identities shape DMS exposure and its health consequences. It will advance measurement by moving beyond additive models to test interactive effects. The findings will directly inform the refinement of clinician training and digital phenotyping tools (e.g., for suicide risk) to be more responsive to intersectional lived experiences.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by this researcher and others has examined structural racism or DMS broadly, this proposal uniquely centers the *interaction* of race and disability—an understudied intersection in healthcare discrimination research—and integrates quantitative effect modification tests with rich qualitative data on mechanisms.

proposals.title: Linking Provider Implicit Bias to Patient-Reported Discrimination: A Multi-Level Analysis of Structural Racism in Adolescent Mental Health Care
proposals.motivation: Structural racism in healthcare contributes to mental health disparities among minoritized youth, but the mechanisms linking provider-level bias to patient outcomes are not fully quantified. Prior to 2026, research has largely examined explicit bias or patient-reported discrimination (DMS) in isolation, creating a critical gap in understanding how implicit cognitive processes among providers directly manifest in clinical encounters and health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stronger implicit racial bias among healthcare providers, as measured by the Implicit Association Test (IAT), will be significantly associated with higher levels of patient-reported discrimination in mental health services (DMS) and poorer mental health outcomes among Black and Latinx adolescents, even after adjusting for structural and practice-level factors.
proposals.method: We will recruit a cohort of 150 mental health providers from a community health system and administer a Race Attitude IAT via a secure digital platform. IAT D-scores will be calculated using the standard Greenwald et al. (2003) algorithm. These scores will be linked, via de-identified provider IDs, to electronic health record data and longitudinal patient-reported outcomes from a sample of 450 adolescent patients (aged 12-17), including the Everyday Discrimination Scale (DMS) and PHQ-9/GAD-7 scores. We will employ multi-level mixed-effects regression models to test the association between provider IAT score and patient outcomes, controlling for patient demographics, provider characteristics, and clinic-level variables.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel empirical evidence directly linking a validated measure of provider implicit bias to patient experiences of discrimination in a real-world clinical setting. It will advance the field beyond documenting associations at the population level to identifying a specific, measurable mechanism (provider implicit cognition) that may perpetuate inequities, informing the development of more targeted clinical bias interventions.
proposals.novelty: While IATs have been used in lab studies, their application to directly link provider cognition to patient-reported discrimination metrics in a live community health system, particularly for adolescent mental health, is novel. This bridges psychological measurement with health services research on structural racism.

proposals.title: Identifying Exemplary Providers: A Mixed-Methods Study of Clinicians with Exceptionally Low Disrespect, Mistreatment, and Stereotyping (DMS) Reports from Racially Minoritized Youth in Community Health Settings
proposals.motivation: Structural racism and discrimination in healthcare settings are well-documented drivers of health inequities, particularly for youth of African descent, leading to adverse mental and physical health outcomes. Prior research, including Dr. Williams's own work, has focused on measuring the negative impacts of discrimination, but less is known about the characteristics and practices of providers who successfully avoid perpetuating these harms, creating a gap in actionable, strengths-based solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that providers with exceptionally low DMS reports from disadvantaged patient groups employ distinct, identifiable interpersonal, clinical, and organizational practices that buffer against the manifestation of structural racism in clinical encounters.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design within a community health system. First, using electronic health record and patient-reported outcome data (including digital phenotyping tools for youth experience), we will apply anomaly detection algorithms (e.g., Isolation Forest) to identify outlier providers with statistically lower DMS event rates among Black and Latino youth panels, controlling for casemix. Second, we will conduct in-depth interviews and ethnographic observations with these identified providers and their patients to characterize their protective practices, communication styles, and clinic environments. Analysis will integrate quantitative outlier profiles with qualitative thematic analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from documenting harm to identifying concrete, replicable protective factors within clinical practice. It will contribute a novel, data-driven framework for identifying 'exemplary providers' and generate a taxonomy of actionable strategies to reduce discrimination in clinical settings, directly informing intervention development and clinician training.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the dominant deficit-focused paradigm in discrimination research by using computational anomaly detection to identify positive outliers, and uniquely links quantitative outlier identification with rich qualitative inquiry to understand the mechanisms of their success.

proposals.title: Structural Racism in the Healthcare Bureaucracy: A Mixed-Methods Investigation of Discrimination in Pharmacies and Insurance Offices and Associations with Youth Mental Health Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Research on discrimination in healthcare has focused predominantly on clinical encounters, yet critical non-clinical settings like pharmacies and insurance offices are key structural nodes that gatekeep access to medication, coverage, and services. Prior to 2026, evidence suggests these administrative touchpoints are potential sites of racialized bias, but systematic investigation is lacking. This gap limits our understanding of how structural racism operates across the entire health ecosystem to impact minoritized youth.
proposals.hypothesis: Experiences of discrimination in non-clinical health settings (pharmacies, insurance offices) are prevalent among racially minoritized youth and their caregivers, and these experiences are independently associated with adverse mental health outcomes, even after accounting for discrimination in clinical settings.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent mixed-methods design. First, a quantitative survey of 500 minoritized youth (ages 13-21) and their caregivers will measure discrimination frequency and stressor severity in specific non-clinical settings using adapted items from the Everyday Discrimination Scale and the Discrimination in Health Care Scale. Mental health outcomes (depressive symptoms, anxiety, suicidal ideation) will be measured via validated scales (PHQ-9, GAD-7). We will use multivariable regression models to test associations, controlling for clinical discrimination and socio-demographics. Second, qualitative interviews (n=40) will provide narrative depth on discriminatory mechanisms and impacts.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the first dedicated scale and dataset mapping discrimination across the non-clinical health bureaucracy. It will quantify the unique mental health burden imposed by these administrative encounters, moving beyond a clinical-centric model. Findings will inform targeted anti-racism interventions for pharmacy staff and insurance administrators.
proposals.novelty: This shifts the locus of inquiry from the doctor's office to the understudied administrative and commercial spaces that are critical for healthcare access. It explicitly tests whether discrimination in these settings has an independent effect on youth mental health, a novel structural pathway.

proposals.title: Beyond Harm: A Mixed-Methods Study of Coping Responses to Discrimination as Moderators of Mental Health Outcomes Among Urban Youth of Color
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research robustly documents the deleterious health effects of structural and interpersonal discrimination, particularly for youth of color. However, a singular focus on harm may inadvertently obscure understanding of resilience and the specific, modifiable coping mechanisms that can buffer these effects. This gap limits the development of targeted, strengths-based interventions to promote mental health equity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, active coping responses to discrimination (e.g., identity affirmation, social support seeking, critical action) will significantly moderate the association between discrimination exposure and mental health symptoms, predicting lower levels of depression and anxiety and higher psychological well-being among youth of color.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal, explanatory sequential mixed-methods design. First, a quantitative survey of 400 youth (aged 14-19) will measure discrimination exposure (using the Everyday Discrimination Scale), coping responses (using the Coping with Discrimination Scale), and mental health outcomes (PHQ-9, GAD-7, Ryff's Scales). We will use hierarchical linear regression and moderation analysis to test associations. Second, we will purposively sample 30 youth from the survey for in-depth qualitative interviews, informed by digital phenotyping data from a mobile app (refined from prior work) to capture real-time coping and mood, to explore the lived experience and mechanisms of resilient coping.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from documenting harm to identifying specific, actionable pathways to resilience. It will produce a nuanced model of discrimination-coping-outcome pathways and generate preliminary evidence for modifiable intervention targets. Findings will inform the development of culturally grounded, asset-focused mental health promotion programs.
proposals.novelty: This work directly inverts the dominant deficit-framework in discrimination research by centering resilience and empirically testing coping as a moderator, not just a mediator, of health outcomes. It integrates structural racism frameworks with developmental psychopathology methods to identify protective processes within marginalized groups.

proposals.title: Beyond Income and Education: A High-Dimensional Mediation Analysis of Socioeconomic Complexity in Racial Health Disparities Among Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on racial health disparities often treats socioeconomic status (SES) as a confounder to be adjusted for, using coarse proxies like income or education. This approach risks underestimating the full explanatory power of structural inequities, potentially reinforcing a deficit narrative by implying residual disparities are due to inherent racial differences. A more nuanced, multidimensional measurement of socioeconomic context is needed to rigorously test whether structural factors fully mediate observed racial gaps in youth mental and physical health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-dimensional latent construct of socioeconomic context—encompassing wealth, debt, neighborhood opportunity, environmental stressors, and economic precarity—will fully statistically mediate the association between self-identified race and adverse health outcomes in a national adolescent cohort, whereas traditional SES measures will not.
proposals.method: We will analyze data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, employing structural equation modeling (SEM). The primary exposure is caregiver-reported race/ethnicity. The mediator is a latent socioeconomic construct defined by 15+ indicators (e.g., family wealth, food/housing insecurity, neighborhood ADI, credit score, subjective social status). Outcomes include caregiver/youth-reported mental health scores and biometrics. We will use a causal mediation framework (R `mediation` package) to decompose total effects into direct (racial) and indirect (SES-mediated) effects, comparing models with traditional vs. high-dimensional SES.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous, quantitative test of the sufficiency of structural socioeconomic factors in explaining racial health disparities. It will advance methodological standards for measuring structural racism in health research and offer evidence to shift policy debates from individual-level interventions toward structural remediation. The findings will directly inform Dr. Williams's work on digital phenotyping in minoritized youth by clarifying the foundational social determinants to be accounted for.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond the standard practice of 'controlling for SES,' this study explicitly models socioeconomic context as a high-dimensional mediator, testing its sufficiency rather than its necessity. This formalizes a core tenet of structural racism theory—that racial disparities are fully attributable to unjust systems—into a testable quantitative hypothesis using pre-2026, state-of-the-art causal inference methods.

proposals.title: Capturing the Immediate Biopsychosocial Impact: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study of Discrimination Exposure and Physiological Reactivity in Minoritized Youth
proposals.motivation: Structural racism and discrimination are established social determinants of health, with profound implications for mental and physical outcomes among youth of color. However, pre-2026 research largely relies on retrospective self-reports, which are subject to recall bias and cannot capture the real-time, within-person dynamics linking discrete discrimination events to immediate physiological and affective responses. This gap limits our mechanistic understanding of how discrimination 'gets under the skin' to affect health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to racial/ethnic discrimination events, captured in real-time via Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), will be associated with concurrent increases in physiological arousal (measured via wearable heart rate and electrodermal activity) and negative affect, and that these associations will be stronger for youth reporting higher levels of prior structural racism exposure.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 Black and Latinx adolescents (aged 15-18) from community health settings. Participants will complete a baseline survey on structural racism exposure. For 14 days, they will respond to 4-5 random EMA prompts daily assessing discrimination events, affect, and context via a smartphone app. Concurrently, they will wear a commercially available wearable device (e.g., Empatica E4) to continuously capture physiological data (heart rate, heart rate variability, electrodermal activity). We will use time-synchronized multilevel modeling to analyze the within-person association between EMA-reported discrimination and physiological reactivity, controlling for activity and time of day.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel, high-resolution data on the real-time biopsychosocial pathways of discrimination. It will advance the field beyond cross-sectional associations by modeling dynamic, within-person processes. The findings will directly inform the development of just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) aimed at mitigating the acute stress impact of discrimination for minoritized youth.
proposals.novelty: While EMA and wearables have been used separately, their integrated, time-synchronized application to capture the immediate physiological sequelae of discrimination events in adolescents' natural environments is novel. This approach moves from assessing 'between-person' associations to modeling 'within-person' causal processes as they unfold.

proposals.title: Associations Between Structural Racism, Discrimination Stress, and Psychoneuroimmunological Dysregulation in Adolescents of African Descent: A Community-Based Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research establishes that structural racism is a critical determinant of health disparities, with significant impacts on adolescent development and mental health. However, the specific biological pathways linking lived experiences of discrimination to poor health outcomes remain inadequately quantified. Applying psychoneuroimmunology (PNI) methods offers a mechanistic lens to understand how social stressors become biologically embedded, a gap particularly salient for minoritized youth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher self-reported and ecologically assessed experiences of racial discrimination will be associated with a dysregulated psychoneuroimmunological profile, characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α) and a flattened diurnal cortisol slope, and that these associations will be moderated by neighborhood-level structural racism indicators.
proposals.method: We will recruit a longitudinal cohort of 200 adolescents (aged 14-18) of African descent from a community health system. Discrimination exposure will be measured via a validated self-report scale and ecological momentary assessment (EMA) via a smartphone app, building on Dr. Williams's prior digital phenotyping work. Salivary cortisol will be collected four times daily over three days to model diurnal rhythm. Fasting blood samples will be analyzed for inflammatory markers (IL-6, TNF-α, CRP) at baseline and 12-month follow-up. We will use multilevel linear regression models to test the association between discrimination (EMA and survey) and biological outcomes, controlling for covariates. Structural equation modeling will test mediation pathways linking structural factors (via geocoded indices of racial inequality) to biological dysregulation through discrimination.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel, longitudinal evidence on the psychoneuroimmunological mechanisms linking structural and interpersonal racism to health in a critical developmental period. It will advance the field by integrating subjective, ecological, and biological measures of stress within a structural framework, offering concrete biomarkers for evaluating interventions aimed at mitigating the health impacts of racism.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely integrates community-based digital phenotyping of discrimination with a comprehensive PNI biomarker panel and geospatial measures of structural racism, moving beyond cross-sectional, single-marker studies to model dynamic, multi-system biological stress processes in adolescents.

proposals.title: Mapping the Unseen: A Bayesian Spatial Inverse Model to Infer Structural Racism from Pediatric Health Disparity Data
proposals.motivation: Structural racism is a fundamental cause of health inequities, but its spatial distribution is often inferred qualitatively or via proxy measures like redlining maps. Pre-2026, there is a critical gap in methods to systematically identify contemporary geographic patterns of structural racism directly from observed health outcomes. This limits targeted policy and intervention, especially for youth mental and physical health in community health systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial patterning of multi-domain pediatric health disparities (e.g., asthma, preterm birth, suicide risk) contains a latent signal that can be statistically separated from other socio-economic confounders and used to infer a high-probability, geographically specific index of structural racism impact.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian hierarchical spatial model treating structural racism as a latent spatial field. Using high-resolution, small-area data (e.g., census tracts) on youth health outcomes from community health records, we will model these outcomes as a function of observed covariates (income, education) plus the latent field. The model will use an intrinsic conditional autoregressive (ICAR) prior for spatial smoothing of the latent field. Inference will be performed using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo via Stan to estimate the posterior probability that a given area's health disparities are attributable to the latent factor (structural racism).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, algorithmically derived 'racism impact map' for use by public health officials and community organizations. It contributes a formal quantitative framework for the 'inverse problem' in health equity research, moving from documenting disparities to probabilistically identifying their root structural cause. It directly extends Dr. Williams's work on digital phenotyping and structural racism's impact on youth by offering a new spatial analytics tool.
proposals.novelty: While many studies associate place-based factors with health, the explicit treatment of structural racism as a spatially structured latent variable to be inferred from multi-outcome disparity data is a novel computational epidemiology approach. It goes beyond using historical proxies to estimate present-day impact.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity in Mental Health Outcomes Among Black American Youth: A Person-Centered Analysis of Intersectional Risk and Protective Factors
proposals.motivation: Public health and clinical research often treat broad racial/ethnic categories as homogeneous, potentially obscuring critical within-group variation in mental health risk. This is particularly salient for Black American youth, a population disproportionately exposed to structural racism but also characterized by diverse lived experiences, ethnic backgrounds, and socioeconomic contexts. Failing to account for this heterogeneity can lead to ineffective, one-size-fits-all interventions and perpetuate health inequities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, empirically derived subgroups exist within a sample of Black American youth, characterized by unique profiles of intersectional factors (e.g., ethnic identity, caregiver support, neighborhood cohesion, discrimination type/frequency), and that these subgroups will demonstrate significantly different patterns of mental health outcomes, including suicidal ideation, depression, and anxiety.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal cohort data (e.g., ABCD Study, Fragile Families) and/or collect new data via a digital phenotyping app refined from Williams' prior work to measure mental health symptoms and contextual exposures. We will employ Latent Profile Analysis (LPA) and Latent Class Analysis (LCA) to identify unobserved subgroups based on a suite of sociodemographic, psychosocial, and environmental variables. The association between class membership and mental health outcomes will be tested using structural equation modeling (SEM), controlling for relevant confounders and examining effect modification by age and gender.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond documenting average between-group disparities to map the specific constellations of risk and resilience within a marginalized population. It will provide a more nuanced, person-centered evidence base to inform the development of targeted, culturally attuned mental health prevention strategies. The findings will challenge the field's reliance on monolithic racial categories in etiological research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes associations between structural racism and health, this study uniquely applies person-centered analytic techniques to de-homogenize the 'Black youth' category, explicitly modeling how multiple intersecting social positions co-occur to shape mental health differentials. It integrates digital phenotyping for outcome measurement with advanced latent variable modeling for subgroup discovery.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Differential Effectiveness of School-Based Mental Health Interventions for Black and Latinx Youth: A Quasi-Experimental Study of Structural Racism as a Moderator
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 data shows alarming and disproportionate increases in poor mental health outcomes, including suicidal ideation, among racially minoritized youth. This crisis is driven by structural racism, yet most intervention evaluations do not explicitly test whether program effectiveness is moderated by community-level structural inequities, potentially widening disparities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effectiveness of established school-based mental health interventions (e.g., CBITS, trauma-informed care models) will be significantly attenuated for Black and Latinx youth in communities with higher measurable structural racism (e.g., residential segregation, police violence rates), compared to youth in communities with lower levels.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, longitudinal quasi-experimental study using a difference-in-differences design. We will link individual-level longitudinal mental health data (from digital phenotyping apps and clinical records) from community health systems with geocoded, public data on structural racism indicators (e.g., racial economic inequality from the ACS, redlining maps from HOLC). Moderated mediation analysis using structural equation modeling (SEM) will test whether community-level structural factors moderate the pathway between intervention exposure and changes in mental health symptoms over 12 months.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond documenting associations to identify *for whom and under what conditions* interventions fail or succeed, providing actionable evidence for tailoring policies. It will advance methods for integrating macro-level structural measures with micro-level clinical and digital phenotyping data to inform equity-focused implementation science.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links structural racism to health outcomes, few studies empirically test it as a direct moderator of intervention efficacy. This proposal innovatively combines Rudyard's expertise in digital phenotyping for minoritized youth with causal inference methods to evaluate intervention effectiveness in a real-world, structurally stratified context.

proposals.title: Causal Effects of State-Level Anti-Discrimination Policies on Adolescent Mental Health: A Quasi-Experimental Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Williams's own work, establishes strong associations between structural racism and adverse mental health outcomes among youth of color. However, a critical gap exists in causally evaluating whether specific policy interventions can mitigate these harms. As states enact varied legislation addressing discrimination in education and policing, a rigorous causal analysis is urgently needed to inform evidence-based policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the enactment of state-level policies aimed at reducing structural discrimination (e.g., school-based anti-bias training mandates, bans on hair-based discrimination) will causally reduce symptoms of depression and anxiety among Black and Hispanic adolescents, with effects measurable in population-level mental health data.
proposals.method: We will employ a difference-in-differences (DiD) design, leveraging the staggered implementation of anti-discrimination policies across U.S. states from 2015-2025. The primary outcome will be adolescent mental health indicators (e.g., PHQ-9, GAD-7 scores) extracted from de-identified electronic health records in a large community health system. We will use a two-way fixed effects estimator, controlling for state and year effects, and conduct robustness checks, including event-study plots and placebo tests, to validate the parallel trends assumption.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond documenting associations to provide quasi-experimental evidence on the mental health efficacy of concrete policy tools. It will directly inform advocacy and legislative efforts by identifying which policy levers are most impactful. Furthermore, it will demonstrate the utility of econometric causal inference methods for evaluating structural determinants of health in a real-world clinical data setting.
proposals.novelty: While DiD is common in economics, its application to evaluate the mental health impacts of anti-discrimination policy using granular clinical data is novel. This work uniquely bridges Dr. Williams's expertise in structural racism, youth mental health, and digital phenotyping with rigorous causal inference to answer a pressing policy-relevant question.

proposals.title: Mapping Structural Racism and Adolescent Mental Health: A Geospatial Analysis of Hotspots in a Community Health System
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Williams's own work, establishes that structural racism is a critical determinant of health disparities, particularly for adolescents of African descent. However, the spatial patterning of these inequities in mental health outcomes within community health systems remains inadequately mapped, limiting targeted intervention. This study addresses a critical gap by applying geospatial techniques to visualize and analyze the concentration of poor mental health outcomes in relation to neighborhood-level indicators of structural disadvantage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant spatial clusters (hotspots) of elevated adolescent mental health distress will be identified within a community health system and that these hotspots will be geographically co-located with census tract-level proxies for structural racism (e.g., racial residential segregation, poverty density, police stop data).
proposals.method: We will utilize anonymized electronic health record (EHR) data from a community health system, extracting geocoded addresses and mental health screening scores (e.g., PHQ-9, GAD-7) for adolescents (ages 12-18). Using Geographic Information Systems (GIS), we will perform spatial autocorrelation analysis (Global and Local Moran's I) to identify statistically significant hotspots of poor mental health. These clusters will then be analyzed via spatial regression against tract-level data from the American Community Survey (e.g., racial composition, income inequality) and other public datasets capturing policing and housing inequities.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, spatially explicit visualization of mental health inequities for minoritized youth, directly linking clinical data to community-level structural factors. It will contribute a methodological framework for health systems to use EHR data for equity-focused geospatial surveillance. The findings are intended to inform place-based interventions and resource allocation to disrupt the pathways linking structural racism to adolescent mental health.
proposals.novelty: While geospatial analysis is used in public health, its application is novel in integrating clinical mental health data from a community health system with multi-domain indicators of structural racism to map actionable hotspots, moving beyond individual-level associations to a contextual, ecological analysis aligned with Dr. Williams's focus on structural determinants.

proposals.title: Predicting Adolescent Mental Health Risk Using Ensemble Machine Learning: Integrating Digital Phenotyping with Structural and Racial Discrimination Measures
proposals.motivation: Minoritized youth, particularly those of African descent, face disproportionate risks for poor mental health outcomes due to structural racism and discrimination, yet predictive models often fail to capture these complex, multi-level determinants. Current risk assessment tools in community health settings are largely reactive and lack the granularity to account for lived experiences and dynamic behavioral data. This gap limits the ability to proactively allocate resources and tailor interventions for the most vulnerable populations.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble machine learning model that integrates individual-level digital phenotyping data (e.g., from smartphone apps) with community-level structural racism indicators will significantly outperform traditional models in predicting near-term risk of poor mental health outcomes among minoritized youth.
proposals.method: We will develop a stacked ensemble model, using a training dataset from a community health system that includes longitudinal digital phenotyping data (e.g., speech patterns, activity, app usage) from a previously refined app, linked to geocoded structural data (e.g., neighborhood deprivation, policing density, segregation indices). Base learners will include gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), random forests, and a recurrent neural network to model temporal sequences. A meta-learner (logistic regression) will combine their predictions. Model performance will be evaluated using AUC-ROC and calibrated for clinical utility.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, ethically considered predictive tool that explicitly models structural determinants of mental health, moving beyond individual-centric risk factors. It will provide a methodological framework for integrating multi-level data in public health informatics and offer actionable insights for community health systems to target preventive mental health services more equitably.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been applied to digital phenotyping, its integration with validated, geospatial measures of structural racism for predictive modeling in adolescent mental health is novel. The ensemble approach is specifically designed to capture non-linear interactions between dynamic individual behaviors and static structural exposures that are missed by standard regression techniques.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Longitudinal Impact of Co-Created Conceptual Models on Anti-Racist Policy Adoption and Community Mental Health Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Community-based participatory research (CBPR) is a gold standard for developing conceptual models of structural racism and health, but there is a critical evidence gap regarding whether these models tangibly influence policy or improve community well-being over time. This is especially urgent for minoritized youth, where policies shaped by structural racism directly harm mental health, yet the long-term efficacy of co-creation as an intervention remains unmeasured.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the depth of community co-creation in developing structural racism models is positively associated with (a) the subsequent adoption of anti-racist policies in local institutions and (b) measurable improvements in community-level mental health outcomes over a multi-year period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, mixed-methods study linking historical CBPR projects (2015-2025) to policy and health data. Using a natural language processing pipeline (BERT-based classifiers) on policy documents and meeting minutes, we will quantify policy adoption. Community mental health outcomes will be assessed via aggregated, de-identified digital phenotyping data (passive sensing and ecological momentary assessment) from community health systems, analyzing trends using interrupted time-series and difference-in-differences models. Qualitative comparative analysis (QCA) will identify necessary conditions in the co-creation process for successful policy impact.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, empirical evidence linking the process of community co-creation to concrete policy and health outcomes. It will advance methods for evaluating structural interventions and offer a validated framework for funders and community partners to design more impactful participatory research aimed at dismantling structural racism.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond documenting associations or short-term process outcomes to causally modeling the long-term impact of the research *process itself* on systemic change, integrating computational policy analysis with community-sourced digital health data in a novel way.

proposals.title: Mapping the Logic of Disparity: A Computational-Ethnographic Audit of Care Pathways in a Pediatric Community Health System
proposals.motivation: Structural racism in healthcare is well-documented as a driver of inequitable outcomes, but research often stops at labeling systems as biased without detailing the operational mechanisms. Pre-2026, there is a critical gap in methods that concretely link institutional data architectures and clinical protocols to the production of racialized disparities in real-time care delivery, particularly for youth. This study addresses the need to move from association to mechanism, identifying precise intervention points within system logics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that racial disparities in pediatric care outcomes within a community health system are produced not by uniform bias, but by specific, identifiable choke points in institutional data flows and protocol-driven decision-making that systematically constrain options for minoritized youth.
proposals.method: We will conduct a computational audit of de-identified electronic health record and administrative data (e.g., referral patterns, wait times, medication orders) using pre-2026 disparity measurement algorithms (e.g., Bayesian regression, causal forests) to flag outlier pathways. Concurrently, we will perform embedded ethnography at flagged decision-nodes (e.g., triage, care coordination meetings) to observe how protocols, algorithmic suggestions, and resource constraints shape clinician judgments. Data integration will use a mixed-methods matrix to align statistical anomalies with observed institutional logics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a mechanistic map of a healthcare system, identifying specific policy, algorithmic, and procedural loci where inequities are generated. It will advance methodological integration for health equity research and provide actionable evidence for system administrators to redesign protocols, moving beyond implicit bias training to structural intervention.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal coupling of computational disparity auditing with real-time institutional ethnography at statistically identified decision-points, moving from documenting 'what' disparities exist to explaining 'how' they are procedurally manufactured within a system's everyday operations.

proposals.title: Modeling Intersectional Pathways to Negative Health Experiences: A Network Interaction Approach Adapted from Systems Biology
proposals.motivation: Public health and social science research, particularly on minoritized youth, often struggles to attribute adverse outcomes to specific structural factors like racism, sexism, or classism, treating them as competing or additive explanations. This limits effective intervention. Pre-2026, the field increasingly recognizes intersectionality but lacks formal, quantitative methods to model the non-additive, synergistic interactions between these multi-level social determinants, analogous to the pre-systems biology era's search for single disease causes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that racism, gender, professional hierarchy, and immigration status interact in specific, quantifiable non-additive ways (synergistic or antagonistic) to configure distinct experiential and health outcome pathways, and that these interaction networks can be formally mapped and validated.
proposals.method: We will adapt systems biology network inference methods (e.g., Bayesian networks, interaction term regression with regularization like LASSO) to social science data. Using existing longitudinal datasets on youth mental health (e.g., ABCD Study) and novel digital phenotyping data on experiences, we will treat social identity and structural factors as nodes. The model will estimate conditional dependencies and interaction coefficients to map pathways leading to specific negative outcomes, moving beyond simple mediation or additive models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for causal attribution in intersectional health research, shifting the question from 'which factor' to 'which interaction pathway.' It will produce empirically testable interaction networks that can inform more precise, multi-target interventions to mitigate structural harms experienced by minoritized youth.
proposals.novelty: The proposal is novel in its direct methodological translation of systems biology network modeling to the problem of intersectional social causation, explicitly quantifying interaction effects between racism and other structural factors rather than treating them as separate or additive.

proposals.title: Intersectional Disentanglement: Quantifying the Unique and Joint Contributions of Racism, Gender, and Professional Status to Negative Health Experiences in a Community Health System
proposals.motivation: Public health research often attributes negative health experiences among minoritized populations primarily to racism. However, this singular focus may obscure the complex interplay with other structural factors like gender discrimination and professional hierarchy, limiting the precision of interventions. Understanding the distinct and synergistic contributions of these intersecting axes of marginalization is critical for developing targeted, effective policies and clinical practices to improve mental and physical health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while racism is a significant predictor of negative health-related experiences, its effect is substantially moderated and confounded by gender and professional status (e.g., patient vs. junior staff vs. senior clinician). The perceived attribution of negative experiences 'solely' to racism will vary significantly across these intersecting identities.
proposals.method: We will leverage and extend the digital phenotyping infrastructure from prior work to collect ecological momentary assessment (EMA) data from a stratified sample of African American/Black youth, caregivers, and health system staff. Using multilevel structural equation modeling (SEM), we will construct latent variables for experiences of racism, gender discrimination, and professional disrespect. We will then employ dominance analysis and relative weight analysis to statistically decompose the unique, joint, and relative contributions of each factor to variance in outcomes like psychological distress, care satisfaction, and occupational burnout.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, quantitative framework for intersectional health research, moving beyond assessing associations to disentangling the proportional impact of co-occurring structural determinants. It will yield actionable insights for health systems on whether anti-racism initiatives must be integrated with, for example, gender equity and professional culture reforms to be fully effective.
proposals.novelty: While intersectionality is a well-established theoretical framework, its empirical application in health services research often lacks methods to quantify the distinct contribution of each axis. This study's novelty lies in applying advanced variance decomposition techniques to real-time, multi-population data within an operational health system to test a specific claim about causal attribution.

proposals.title: Unpacking the Black Box: A Realist Evaluation of School-Based Anti-Racism Interventions for Youth Mental Health Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Structural racism is a critical determinant of health inequities, particularly for youth of African descent, impacting development, brain structure, and mental health. However, evaluations of anti-racism interventions, like implicit bias training, often show mixed or null effects, failing to explain why they work in some contexts but not others. This gap limits the development of effective, scalable strategies to mitigate racism's harms on adolescent well-being.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effectiveness of anti-racism program components (resources) in improving youth mental health and reducing discriminatory behaviors is contingent on specific, measurable contextual factors (e.g., organizational readiness, trust in leadership) which enable or inhibit key participant mechanisms (e.g., sense of accountability, perceived skill efficacy).
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site realist evaluation of an existing school-based anti-racism and mental health promotion program. First, we will develop initial Context-Mechanism-Outcome (CMO) configurations via stakeholder interviews and document review. We will then empirically test these configurations using a longitudinal, convergent mixed-methods design: qualitative data (interviews, focus groups) will refine theories of mechanism activation, while quantitative data (pre-post surveys on attitudes, behavioral incident reports, and digital phenotyping app data on mood/behavior from a subsample) will test associations between context, proposed mechanisms, and outcomes using multi-level structural equation modeling to assess moderation and mediation.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from asking 'does it work?' to a nuanced theory explaining 'what works, for which students, under what school conditions, and how?' It will provide a replicable framework for evaluating complex anti-racism interventions and deliver concrete, context-specific guidance for schools and community health systems aiming to implement effective strategies to support minoritized youth.
proposals.novelty: While realist evaluation is established in public health, its systematic application to anti-racism interventions, particularly those targeting youth mental health outcomes, is novel. This approach uniquely integrates mechanisms of individual reasoning with structural and organizational contexts, bridging psychological and sociological levels of analysis.

proposals.title: A Common Garden Approach to Structural Racism: Developing a Standardized Phenotyping Framework for a Multi-National Study of Internationally Recruited Nurses
proposals.motivation: Internationally recruited nurses, often from racialized groups, face significant racism impacting mental health and workforce retention, but comparative research is hindered by methodological heterogeneity. Pre-2026 work highlights the profound impact of structural racism on health and the need for scalable measurement tools, especially for minoritized populations. A standardized, multi-national study is critical to isolate the core experience of racism from varying national contexts to inform effective, transferable interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a standardized 'common garden' measurement framework will allow for the valid cross-national comparison of racism experiences and their mental health sequelae among internationally recruited nurses, revealing universal core stressors distinct from nation-specific contextual moderators.
proposals.method: We will adapt the phytogeography analogy by developing a core phenotyping protocol using validated instruments (e.g., Everyday Discrimination Scale, PHQ-9, GAD-7, resilience scales) deployed digitally via a secure app-based platform. This protocol will be administered to cohorts of 300-500 nurses from similar source countries (e.g., Philippines, India) working in the US, Canada, and Australia. Simultaneously, we will systematically quantify host-country 'environmental' variables (e.g., immigration visa type, hospital equity policies, national discourse metrics from media analysis) to be included as covariates in multi-level structural equation models testing associations between racism exposure and mental health outcomes across sites.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce the first directly comparable, multi-national dataset on nurse racism experiences, moving beyond single-country case studies. It will contribute a novel methodological framework for comparative social science and identify key modifiable contextual factors (e.g., specific policies) that exacerbate or buffer the impact of racism, providing actionable targets for healthcare institutions and policymakers.
proposals.novelty: The proposal's novelty lies in its direct conceptual translation of a 'common garden' experimental design from ecology to social epidemiology, creating a rigorous method for disentangling the experience of racism from its national context, which has not been attempted in health disparities research on this population.

proposals.title: Engineering Safer Environments: A Mixed-Methods Study Applying the Hierarchy of Controls to Mitigate Patient-Source Racism Against Nurses of Color
proposals.motivation: Patient and family-source racism is a pervasive occupational hazard for minority nurses, linked to adverse mental health outcomes, burnout, and attrition, yet interventions remain focused on individual-level reporting and coping. This gap persists despite pre-2026 evidence that structural racism profoundly shapes health and developmental outcomes. Applying a proven public health framework from occupational safety offers a novel pathway to design systemic protections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting and implementing engineering control principles (e.g., environmental redesign, process isolation) within clinical workflows will be associated with reduced self-reported exposure to racist hostility and improved psychological safety among nurses of color, compared to units relying solely on administrative controls or personal protective equipment (PPE) analogs like de-escalation training.
proposals.method: We will conduct a sequential mixed-methods study in a community health system. First, qualitative interviews and focus groups with nurses and administrators will identify modifiable environmental and procedural factors (e.g., triage protocols, team-based patient assignment, physical layout of nursing stations). Second, we will develop and pilot-test specific engineering control interventions on selected units. Third, using a pre-post design, we will measure outcomes via validated scales for workplace discrimination, psychological distress, and occupational safety climate, employing multivariable regression to test associations while controlling for unit and nurse characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This research will generate a novel, actionable framework for classifying and designing structural interventions against interpersonal racism in healthcare workplaces. It will produce preliminary evidence for environmental and procedural modifications that protect nurse well-being, shifting the onus from individual resilience to institutional responsibility.
proposals.novelty: While the hierarchy of controls is standard in occupational health, its formal application to the psychosocial hazard of racism is novel. This moves beyond documenting associations or training individuals to proactively engineer safer systems, a structural approach distinct from prior anti-racism and workplace climate research in nursing.

proposals.title: The Clinical Care Climate: Validating a Scale of Systemic Racism Against Nurses and Testing its Association with Hospital-Level Patient Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Structural racism in healthcare is a recognized driver of health inequities, but its mechanisms are often studied at the patient-provider level. Pre-2026 research on nurses, a predominantly female and racially diverse workforce, shows high rates of discrimination, which is linked to burnout and turnover. However, a critical gap exists in measuring this racism as a systemic, institutional climate factor and quantifying its downstream impact on the quality and safety of patient care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a measurable 'clinical care climate' of systemic racism against nurses exists at the hospital level, and that a poorer (more racist) care climate will be significantly associated with worse aggregated patient safety and clinical effectiveness outcomes.
proposals.method: We will adapt validated safety climate survey methodology to develop and psychometrically validate a novel 'Clinical Care Climate Scale' (CCCS) measuring nurses' shared perceptions of institutional racism and equity. Using a multi-site, cross-sectional design, we will administer the CCCS to nurses across 20-30 hospitals. We will then employ multilevel structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the association between hospital-level CCCS scores and objective, aggregated patient outcome metrics (e.g., HACRP scores, CMS mortality/complication measures, patient experience scores) from the same institutions, controlling for hospital structural characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first validated instrument to quantify systemic racism against nurses as an institutional climate. It will provide rigorous, quantitative evidence linking this climate to patient outcomes, shifting the frame from individual stressor to a modifiable system property. Findings will inform targeted institutional interventions to improve nurse equity as a critical pathway to enhancing patient care quality and equity.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel in its direct adaptation of organizational climate theory to the problem of systemic racism in healthcare and in its methodological shift from analyzing individual-level associations to testing hospital-level, climate-to-outcome pathways, creating a new evidence base for system accountability.

proposals.title: Disaggregating Pan-Ethnic Categories: A Multi-Method Study of Heterogeneity in Mental Health Outcomes and Experiences of Discrimination Among Hispanic and Black Youth
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 public health and developmental research often aggregates diverse populations into pan-ethnic categories like 'Hispanic' or 'Black,' masking critical internal variation. This practice can obscure unique risk and resilience factors linked to nationality, immigration generation, and cultural context, potentially perpetuating health inequities by informing one-size-fits-all interventions. This study directly addresses calls to operationalize structural racism and intersectionality by moving beyond monolithic racial/ethnic groupings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant heterogeneity in mental health outcomes and self-reported experiences of discrimination exists within pan-ethnic categories, and that this variation is systematically associated with factors such as national origin, immigration status (e.g., first vs. second generation), and cultural identity.
proposals.method: Leveraging Dr. Williams's expertise in community-based digital phenotyping, we will recruit a diverse sample of Hispanic and Black youth (ages 14-21) from a community health system. We will employ a mixed-methods design: 1) Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) via a smartphone app to collect real-time data on mood, stressors, and microaggressions; 2) Detailed baseline surveys capturing nationality, immigration history, and cultural factors. Analysis will use multilevel modeling to test for between-group differences within pan-ethnic categories and structural equation modeling to examine pathways linking specific cultural/immigration factors to mental health outcomes via differential exposure to discrimination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence against the homogeneity assumption in racial/ethnic health research. It will yield a more nuanced understanding of how structural and interpersonal racism differentially impact sub-groups, informing the development of more targeted and culturally precise mental health interventions and policies for minoritized youth.
proposals.novelty: While disaggregation is recognized as important, few studies integrate high-frequency digital phenotyping with detailed sociocultural granularity to dynamically capture how lived experiences and mental health manifest differently within commonly used census categories.

proposals.title: Structural Racism and Adolescent Mental Health: A Multi-Group Trend Analysis of Disparities Among Asian, Native American, and African Descent Youth
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on structural racism and adolescent health has often focused on Black/White disparities, leaving significant gaps in understanding the mental health outcomes of Asian and Native American youth. These groups face unique forms of discrimination and structural marginalization, yet their experiences are frequently aggregated or omitted from trend analyses, masking critical heterogeneity in health risks and developmental impacts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, time-varying trends in mental health outcomes (e.g., suicidality, depression) exist among Asian, Native American, and African descent adolescents, and that these trends are differentially associated with specific, measurable dimensions of structural racism (e.g., residential segregation, school funding inequities) relevant to each group's lived experience.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, multi-cohort analysis using electronic health record and community survey data from a large community health system (2015-2025). We will employ structural equation modeling (SEM) with multi-group latent growth curve analysis to model mental health outcome trajectories. Key independent variables will be census-tract-level structural racism indicators (e.g., Index of Concentration at the Extremes for economic segregation, school district funding data). We will test for measurement invariance across groups and use time-lagged models to assess associations between structural factors and subsequent mental health trends.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comparative analysis of mental health trends linked to structural racism across these understudied racial/ethnic groups. It will move beyond a Black/White binary to inform more targeted, culturally specific public health interventions and policy recommendations aimed at reducing health inequities for all minoritized youth.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, comparative modeling of distinct structural racism exposures and mental health trajectories for Asian and Native American youth alongside African descent youth, moving beyond a deficit model to identify group-specific protective and risk pathways within a unified analytical framework.

proposals.title: Resilience and Protective Factors Against Alcohol Use Disorder in Racially Minoritized Youth: A Multi-Level, Digital Phenotyping Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on Alcohol Use Disorders (AUDs) disproportionately focuses on risk factors and disparities, particularly the negative impacts of structural racism on minoritized youth. While crucial, this deficit-focused lens overlooks sources of strength and resilience that prevent AUD onset despite exposure to adversity. Understanding these protective factors is essential for developing effective, culturally grounded prevention strategies that move beyond merely documenting harm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, measurable multi-level protective factors—including individual (e.g., future orientation, emotion regulation), family (e.g., caregiver monitoring, cultural socialization), and community (e.g., ethnic-racial identity affirmation, community belonging)—will significantly buffer the association between exposure to structural racism and the development of AUDs requiring treatment among racially minoritized youth.
proposals.method: We will recruit a longitudinal cohort of 400 racially minoritized youth (ages 14-18) and their caregivers from a community health system. Using a refined digital phenotyping app (building on Dr. Williams's prior work), we will collect ecological momentary assessment (EMA) data on daily stressors, protective experiences, and substance use cues. We will integrate this with geocoded structural data (e.g., neighborhood opportunity indices) and annual clinical assessments. Analyses will employ multi-level structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the direct and moderating effects of protective factors on AUD risk trajectories, controlling for known risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will identify actionable, multi-level protective factors that promote resilience against AUDs in minoritized youth, directly informing strength-based prevention programs. It will advance methodological integration by combining digital phenotyping with structural and lived experience data to model resilience processes dynamically. The findings will shift the narrative from purely risk-focused to resilience-oriented in health disparities research.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the standard disparity framework to systematically investigate protection rather than just pathology. It uniquely integrates real-time digital phenotyping with structural racism metrics to model how resilience operates dynamically within ecological contexts of inequality, a novel approach in AUD prevention research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Structural Determinants of Health in Pacific Island Nations: A Quasi-Experimental Analysis of the Renegotiated Compacts of Free Association on Nutrition and Mental Health Outcomes in Palau and the Marshall Islands
proposals.motivation: The Compacts of Free Association (COFA) between the U.S. and the Freely Associated States (FAS) are foundational structural determinants of health, governing aid, migration, and service access. Pre-2026, these compacts were under renegotiation, with significant implications for public health funding and food security in FAS nations like Palau and the Marshall Islands, which face high burdens of diet-related disease and mental health disparities. This study addresses a critical gap in understanding how macro-level policy changes directly impact population health outcomes in these minoritized and historically marginalized communities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that changes in the renegotiated COFA terms, particularly those related to economic assistance and healthcare provisions, will be significantly associated with measurable shifts in population-level nutrition indicators (e.g., food import patterns, rates of diabetes) and mental health outcomes (e.g., adolescent distress, caregiver stress) in Palau and the Marshall Islands.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal, quasi-experimental difference-in-differences design, analyzing health outcome trends before and after COFA policy implementation against comparison groups. We will integrate multi-level data: 1) policy analysis of compact terms; 2) national health survey data on nutrition and mental health; 3) digital phenotyping data on behavioral markers of distress from a sub-sample of youth and caregivers, using the refined app from Williams' prior work. Analysis will use fixed-effects regression models to test associations between policy changes and health outcomes, controlling for covariates, and structural equation modeling to examine pathways linking policy, economic conditions, and health.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first empirical evidence linking specific COFA provisions to health outcomes, directly informing policy advocacy for health-equitable compact terms. It will extend the literature on structural racism and health by applying a structural determinants framework to international policy and Pacific Islander populations. The findings will offer a model for studying how geopolitical agreements impact the health of marginalized youth and communities.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines structural racism's health impacts domestically, this study uniquely applies this lens to an international compact, treating the COFA as a structural determinant. It innovatively combines macro-level policy analysis with micro-level digital phenotyping data to trace the pathway from policy change to individual health and mental outcomes.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Association: A Causal Mediation Analysis of Racial Disparities in Adolescent Mental Health Outcomes Through Structural and Interpersonal Racism
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Williams's own work, robustly documents racial disparities in mental health outcomes among youth. However, a persistent methodological and conceptual flaw treats race as a biological or inherent causal variable, rather than a social construct and proxy for exposure to systemic inequities. This conflation risks reinforcing racial essentialism and obscures the specific, modifiable pathways—such as discrimination and structural disadvantage—that research and policy must address to improve health equity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the statistical association between racial self-identification and adverse mental health outcomes (e.g., depressive symptoms, suicidality) among adolescents will be fully mediated by quantifiable measures of structural racism (e.g., neighborhood opportunity indices, school funding data) and interpersonal racism (e.g., experiences of discrimination). The direct effect of racial category, net of these factors, will be non-significant.
proposals.method: We will leverage a longitudinal cohort of minoritized youth (N~1500, ages 13-18) from a community health system, integrating digital phenotyping app data on mood and behavior with geocoded administrative data (e.g., Child Opportunity Index, police stop records). Using a causal mediation analysis framework with inverse probability weighting to account for confounding, we will formally decompose the total effect of race on mental health outcomes into: 1) an indirect effect mediated by structural/institutional factors, 2) an indirect effect mediated by self-reported discrimination, and 3) the residual direct effect. Sensitivity analyses will assess robustness to unmeasured confounding of mediator-outcome relationships.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous, quantitative demonstration that 'race' does not cause health disparities; racism does. It will advance methodological standards for health disparities research by explicitly modeling race as a social exposure and racism as the pathogenic vector. The findings will offer concrete, evidence-based targets for intervention—structural reform and anti-discrimination policies—rather than vague allusions to 'racial differences.'
proposals.novelty: While mediation is common, this proposal's novelty lies in its explicit causal framing to statistically nullify race as an explanatory variable, its integration of objective structural metrics with real-time digital phenotyping, and its direct challenge to the interpretative norms of regression models where race is often presented as a standalone, causal risk factor.

proposals.title: Beyond Static Categories: A Multi-Dimensional Latent Variable Model of Racialized Experience and Its Association with Adolescent Mental Health Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 public health research predominantly uses static, administrative racial categories (e.g., Black, White) to measure exposure to racism and predict health outcomes. This approach risks ecological fallacy, obscures within-group heterogeneity, and may poorly capture the lived experiences of discrimination and identity central to developmental and mental health, especially for minoritized youth. Critiquing this assumption is essential for advancing health equity research.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-dimensional latent construct of racialized experience, incorporating perceived discrimination, racial identity centrality, and ethnic-racial socialization, will explain significantly more variance in adolescent mental health outcomes (e.g., depression, anxiety, suicidality) than static racial category alone, and will reveal protective and risk pathways masked by categorical analysis.
proposals.method: We will recruit a diverse cohort of 800 adolescents (aged 14-18) from community health settings. Using a digital phenotyping app adapted from prior work, we will collect longitudinal ecological momentary assessment (EMA) data on daily discrimination events and mood. Cross-sectional surveys will measure racial identity (MEIM-R), lifetime discrimination (EDS), and ethnic-racial socialization. We will employ Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) to test a latent variable model where the latent construct 'Racialized Experience' is defined by these indicators. Its association with mental health outcomes will be tested against a model using only a static racial category variable, controlling for socioeconomic status and neighborhood context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, quantifiable framework for moving beyond crude racial proxies in health research. It will offer concrete methodological tools for capturing the mechanisms through which structural racism impacts youth development. Findings will inform more precise, culturally responsive mental health interventions tailored to individuals' experiences rather than assumed group averages.
proposals.novelty: While the limitations of racial categories are acknowledged, few studies operationalize a multi-dimensional alternative in quantitative health outcomes research. This proposal uniquely integrates digital phenotyping for real-time discrimination assessment with latent variable modeling to directly test the sufficiency of static categories, bridging critical theory with advanced quantitative methods.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Multidimensional Disparity: A Direct Test of the Assumption of Equally Distributed Social Factors Across Racial Groups in Adolescent Health Research
proposals.motivation: Much quantitative health disparities research, especially in youth mental health, implicitly assumes social determinants (e.g., neighborhood safety, economic precarity, discrimination exposure) are similarly distributed across racial groups, serving as a neutral baseline. This assumption, rooted in pre-2026 methodological norms, risks misattributing the effects of structural racism to intrinsic group differences, thereby perpetuating harmful biases in etiological models and intervention targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a comprehensive set of social and environmental factors—spanning neighborhood, economic, educational, and interpersonal domains—are not equally distributed but are systematically clustered by racial group, and that this clustering significantly mediates observed racial disparities in adolescent mental health outcomes.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing longitudinal cohort data (e.g., ABCD Study) using a multi-step approach. First, we will employ latent class analysis (LCA) to identify distinct profiles of social adversity from variables measuring socioeconomic status, neighborhood quality, perceived discrimination, and school context. Second, we will use multinomial logistic regression to test the association between racial group and adversity profile membership. Finally, we will use causal mediation analysis (e.g., parametric g-formula) to quantify the proportion of the association between race and mental health outcomes (e.g., depression, suicidality) explained by these adversity profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical, quantitative evidence against a pervasive methodological assumption, shifting the analytical focus from race as a risk factor to the unequal distribution of contextual risks. It will offer a replicable framework for modeling structural racism as a latent construct in health research and inform more equitable intervention designs by identifying modifiable, upstream social determinants.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes associations between single social factors and health, this study directly tests the foundational assumption of equal distribution across groups using a multidimensional, person-centered modeling approach, moving beyond documenting disparities to deconstructing their constitutive social architecture.
