proposals.title: A Systematic, Multi-Level Catalog of Social Exposures: Mapping the Structural and Interpersonal Factors Differentially Experienced by Racial and Ethnic Groups in the United States
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research on racial health disparities often treats race as a biological variable rather than a social construct, obscuring the specific, modifiable exposures driving inequities. A systematic catalog of the social factors that vary across groups is a critical prerequisite for moving beyond mere association studies to research that identifies precise intervention targets. This is especially urgent for youth development and mental health, where structural racism shapes lived experiences and health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, multi-domain analysis of population-level and administrative data will reveal a distinct, quantifiable profile of social exposures—spanning economic, environmental, institutional, and interpersonal domains—for major racial and ethnic groups in the U.S., providing a concrete map of differential lived experiences.
proposals.method: We will employ a structural equation modeling (SEM) framework to create a multi-level catalog. Using nationally representative pre-2026 datasets (e.g., NHANES, Add Health, ACS), we will identify latent constructs for social factors (e.g., neighborhood deprivation, policing exposure, educational resource inequality, microaggression frequency) through confirmatory factor analysis. We will then use between-group invariance testing to statistically identify which factor structures and mean levels differ significantly across racial/ethnic groups, moving beyond simple descriptive lists to a validated model of differential exposure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, multi-domain catalog of race-associated social exposures, shifting the field from generic discussions of 'racism' to a measurable set of targetable constructs. It will directly inform the design of etiological studies and interventions, such as digital phenotyping tools, by specifying which contextual factors to measure as potential mediators of mental health outcomes among minoritized youth.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior reviews that narratively describe broad domains of racism, this study applies a rigorous, quantitative structural modeling approach to operationalize and test for specific differential exposures across groups, creating a replicable and falsifiable measurement model for use in health disparities research.

proposals.title: Deconstructing Race as a Variable: A Sociological Construct Validation Approach for Measuring Structural Racism in Adolescent Health Research
proposals.motivation: In pre-2026 health disparities research, race is often treated as a crude, static demographic variable, conflating biological, social, and structural dimensions. This limits the ability to precisely measure the mechanisms through which structural racism operates to produce adverse mental and physical health outcomes among youth, as highlighted in Dr. Williams's prior work. Adopting rigorous sociological methods for construct validation is critical to move beyond associations and model the specific, measurable pathways of discrimination.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying sociological construct validation frameworks will yield a multi-dimensional, empirically grounded operationalization of race-related exposures (e.g., spatial segregation, institutional bias, vicarious discrimination) that more strongly predicts variance in mental health outcomes among minoritized youth than traditional race/ethnicity categories alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods study within a community health system. First, using qualitative interviews and focus groups with adolescents of African descent and caregivers, we will identify key domains of structural and lived experience. Second, we will employ confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) and structural equation modeling (SEM) on survey and digital phenotyping data (e.g., ecological momentary assessment of mood/stress) to test the latent structure of the identified constructs. This will validate a measurement model specifying the relationships between observed indicators (e.g., neighborhood poverty, police stops, microaggressions) and the underlying theoretical constructs of structural racism.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, actionable measurement model for structural racism in adolescent health research, moving the field from conceptual models to testable pathways. It will directly inform the refinement of digital phenotyping tools to capture relevant, real-world exposures and strengthen causal inference in studies linking discrimination to brain structure and health outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While the impact of structural racism is recognized, its operationalization in quantitative youth health research remains underdeveloped. This proposal uniquely integrates sociological measurement theory with clinical and digital mental health methods to deconstruct the 'black box' of the race variable, offering a novel analytical framework for the field.

proposals.title: Narratives of Care: A Micro-Level Analysis of Cultural Schemas and Healthcare Utilization Among Racially Minoritized Youth
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Williams's own work, robustly demonstrates the association between structural racism and adverse health outcomes for youth of African descent. However, population-level studies and even digital phenotyping tools may obscure the specific, culturally embedded cognitive schemas and decision-making processes that mediate between structural discrimination and individual healthcare utilization. Understanding these micro-level cultural factors is critical for designing effective, person-centered interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in-depth narrative analysis will reveal a set of distinct, culturally informed schemas (e.g., related to trust, self-reliance, or community protection) that directly shape healthcare-seeking behaviors and engagement with mental health services among racially minoritized youth, and that these schemas are not fully captured by standard measures of discrimination or access.
proposals.method: We will conduct longitudinal, semi-structured narrative interviews with 25-30 racially minoritized youth (ages 16-21) recruited from a community health system, focusing on their healthcare journeys and decision-making. Interviews will be transcribed and analyzed using a mixed-methods approach: (1) Thematic analysis to identify emergent cultural schemas, and (2) application of established Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques from the pre-2026 era (specifically, topic modeling with Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and sentiment analysis using Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner (VADER)) to quantify and validate the prevalence and emotional valence of identified themes across narratives.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel, fine-grained typology of cultural schemas impacting health utilization, moving beyond broad associations to actionable mechanisms. It will provide a methodological blueprint for integrating rich qualitative narrative data with computational NLP tools to study health disparities, offering a model for 'scaling down' to the individual level while maintaining analytical rigor.
proposals.novelty: While narrative methods are common in qualitative health research, the systematic integration of thematic analysis with specific, pre-2026 NLP algorithms (LDA, VADER) to decode cultural schemas in health utilization among this population is novel. This approach bridges the gap between macro-structural analyses and individual lived experience in a computationally grounded way.

proposals.title: Simulating National Policy Levers: A Microsimulation Model of Structural Racism, Social Determinants, and Racial Gaps in Mental Health Service Utilization
proposals.motivation: Persistent racial gaps in mental health service utilization are driven by structural racism, shaping inequitable distributions of social determinants like income, education, and neighborhood safety. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Williams's own work, documents these associations but lacks tools to quantify how population-level shifts in these determinants could alter utilization gaps at a national scale, limiting evidence for macro-level policy interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that simulated national policies which reduce racial inequities in the distributions of key social determinants (e.g., household wealth, police discrimination exposure) will significantly narrow, but not eliminate, modeled racial gaps in mental health service utilization among youth.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic microsimulation model using pre-2026 nationally representative data (e.g., NSDUH, Add Health). The model will incorporate structural equation modules to represent established pathways from structural racism (e.g., redlining indices) to social determinants (e.g., via an agent's education and income algorithms) and then to individual utilization probability via a logistic function. We will test counterfactual scenarios by altering the input distributions of social factors for simulated agents of different races, using variance reduction techniques to estimate the causal effect of these distributional shifts on the population-level utilization gap.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, policy-actionable tool to move beyond identifying associations to simulating the population health impact of structural interventions. It will generate quantitative estimates of how much closing specific socioeconomic gaps could reduce mental health care disparities, offering crucial evidence for policymakers prioritizing national strategies to mitigate structural racism's health effects.
proposals.novelty: While microsimulation is used in health economics, its application to model the effects of structural racism on mental health utilization by explicitly manipulating social factor distributions across racialized groups at a national scale represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance beyond standard regression-based disparity studies.

proposals.title: Inferring Unmeasured Structural Racism from Healthcare Utilization Patterns: A Latent Variable Approach for Adolescent Mental Health Equity
proposals.motivation: Structural racism is a critical determinant of health disparities, but it is notoriously difficult to measure directly in large-scale health system data, often being inferred from static proxies like neighborhood or race/ethnicity. This limits the ability to quantify its specific, dynamic impact on healthcare engagement and mental health outcomes for minoritized youth. Pre-2026, there is a pressing need to develop methods that can reveal the latent social context from observable behaviors like service utilization, especially within community health systems serving vulnerable populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a latent variable model applied to high-dimensional, longitudinal healthcare utilization data (e.g., visit types, frequencies, no-shows, digital app engagement) can recover a robust, person-specific construct of exposure to structural racism, and that this inferred measure will be more strongly associated with adverse mental health outcomes than traditional proxy measures.
proposals.method: We will leverage de-identified electronic health record and digital phenotyping app data from a community health system serving a racially diverse adolescent population. Using a Bayesian structural equation modeling (SEM) framework, we will specify a latent variable representing 'inferred structural racism exposure' as a common cause of multiple observed utilization indicators (e.g., emergency vs. preventive care ratio, appointment adherence, app disengagement patterns). The model will control for measured covariates (e.g., insurance, diagnosis) to isolate the social signal. Model fit will be assessed using comparative fit index (CFI) and RMSEA, and the latent variable's predictive validity will be tested against outcomes like suicidal behavior risk scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven method for quantifying a key unmeasured social determinant of health within operational health system data. It will advance health equity research by moving beyond crude proxies to a more nuanced, person-centered measure of structural racism exposure, directly informing targeted interventions within community health settings to improve mental health outcomes for minoritized youth.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes associations between racism and health, this study innovates by inverting the analytical paradigm: instead of using social measures to predict utilization, we use utilization patterns to infer the latent social context, operationalizing structural racism as a quantifiable force manifesting in healthcare engagement behaviors.

proposals.title: Structural Racism and Adolescent Mental Health: A Comparative Test of Measurement Models in Brazil and South Africa
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on structural racism and youth health, including Dr. Williams's own work, is overwhelmingly U.S.-centric, relying on a Black/White binary and a history of de jure segregation. This limits generalizability and obscures how distinct racial classification systems (e.g., based on colorism or apartheid) shape health mechanisms. Testing models in nations with different racial histories is crucial for developing a global science of structural discrimination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-domain model of structural racism (measuring residential, economic, and political segregation) will significantly associate with adverse mental health outcomes among adolescents of African descent in Brazil and South Africa, but that the relative strength and interaction of these domains will differ from U.S. patterns due to nation-specific racial constructs and policies.
proposals.method: We will conduct cross-sectional surveys with adolescents (aged 14-18) of African descent in São Paulo, Brazil (n=600) and Johannesburg, South Africa (n=600). Structural racism will be measured using multi-level, geo-linked indices (e.g., neighborhood racial composition, income inequality by racial group, and political representation). Mental health outcomes (depressive symptoms, anxiety, suicidality) will be assessed via validated scales and digital phenotyping app prompts. We will use structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the fit of the U.S.-derived measurement model and employ multi-group analysis to compare path coefficients (associations between structural racism domains and mental health) between the two nations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of a structural racism and youth health model outside the U.S. context. It will advance measurement science by identifying which domains of structural discrimination are universally salient versus context-specific. Findings will inform the development of more culturally and historically precise interventions for minoritized youth globally.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond U.S.-specific racial constructs to a comparative framework is novel, as is directly testing the transportability of a structural racism measurement model. The focus on two non-U.S. nations with deeply entrenched but distinct racial hierarchies (Brazil's color continuum and South Africa's legacy of apartheid) allows for a powerful natural experiment.

proposals.title: Disentangling Lived Experience from Ancestry: An Intensive Family Triad Study of Racism, Mental Health, and Digital Phenotyping in Multiracial Families
proposals.motivation: Structural racism is a critical determinant of health disparities, but its specific pathways—whether through direct lived experience, internalized stigma, or biological embedding—remain poorly understood. Studying families where parents and children have different racial identifications or ancestries provides a powerful natural experiment to separate the effects of perceived racialization and discrimination from shared genetic and broader environmental factors, a gap highlighted in pre-2026 reviews on adolescent development and brain health.
proposals.hypothesis: Within such family triads, adolescent mental health outcomes and associated digital phenotyping signatures (e.g., in speech, keystroke dynamics, and mobility) will correlate more strongly with the adolescent's own lived experiences of discrimination and racial identity than with the racial identification or discrimination experiences of their parents.
proposals.method: We will recruit 50-75 family triads (adolescent and two primary caregivers) from community health systems where family members self-report differing racial identities/ancestries. Using a refined digital phenotyping app (based on Williams' prior work), we will collect passive sensor data (GPS, accelerometer, phone use) and active ecological momentary assessments (EMAs) on mood, social interactions, and discrimination events from all participants over 6 months. We will employ multilevel structural equation modeling to partition variance in mental health outcomes (from validated EMA items and clinical records) and computationally derived behavioral markers (e.g., social rhythm disruption, semantic content from speech samples analyzed via NLP) between individual-level discrimination experiences and family-level factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel empirical evidence on the primacy of lived experience in the pathway from racism to health, moving beyond population-level associations. It will also validate a family-based digital phenotyping framework for measuring relational and individual dynamics of minority stress, offering a new methodological approach for health disparities research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines structural racism between groups or in homogeneous families, this design leverages intra-family variation in racialization as a unique control for confounding, allowing a more precise test of how discrimination 'gets under the skin' of adolescents. The integration of intensive digital phenotyping across the family triad is methodologically novel.

proposals.title: The Health-Identity Feedback Loop: Modeling the Influence of Mental and Physical Health Outcomes on Racial Self-Identification and Social Assignment Among Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Research has robustly documented how racial identity and discrimination shape health outcomes, particularly for minoritized youth. However, the inverse pathway—how the experience of specific health conditions might influence racial self-identification or how one is racially perceived by others—remains largely unexplored. This gap is critical in a pre-2026 context where health disparities are widening and identity is increasingly recognized as fluid, with implications for developmental trajectories, service access, and the measurement of health inequities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the onset or chronicity of specific mental and physical health conditions (e.g., depression, anxiety, stress-related disorders) predicts measurable changes in the strength, centrality, or category of racial self-identification among adolescents, and that these changes are moderated by experiences of structural racism and healthcare discrimination.
proposals.method: We will leverage longitudinal cohort data (e.g., Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study) to model health outcomes as time-varying predictors of identity change. Using a structural equation modeling (SEM) framework, we will specify cross-lagged panel models to test temporal precedence and bidirectional associations. Latent transition analysis will be employed to model categorical shifts in self-identification. Key moderators (e.g., neighborhood segregation, caregiver experiences) and mediators (e.g., internalized stigma, social network composition) will be integrated into the structural models.
proposals.contribution: This work will pioneer a novel theoretical framework on health as a driver of identity formation, moving beyond identity as a static confounder. It will provide empirical evidence for a feedback loop between health and racial identity, offering critical nuance for developmental models and for interpreting population health data where identity is measured at a single time point. The findings will inform more dynamic, culturally responsive interventions for youth mental health.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the dominant causal direction in health disparities research by treating health as an explanatory variable for identity, not solely an outcome. It uniquely integrates developmental, sociological, and computational methods to model identity as a dynamic construct responsive to embodied experience.

proposals.title: Mapping Intersectional Resilience: A Digital Phenotyping Study of Strength-Based Coping Among Racially Minoritized and LGBTQ+ Youth
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on minoritized youth overwhelmingly documents health disparities and risks linked to structural racism and discrimination, framing intersectional identities primarily as burdens. This deficit-focused lens overlooks how these identities can foster resilience and positive coping, which are critical but understudied protective factors for mental health. A strength-based investigation is urgently needed to inform interventions that build on existing community assets rather than just mitigating risks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, measurable patterns of digital behavior (e.g., social connectivity, information seeking, creative expression) will be significantly associated with self-reported resilience and positive affect, and that these resilience-signaling phenotypes will be uniquely shaped by the intersection of racial/ethnic and sexual/gender identities.
proposals.method: We will adapt our existing digital phenotyping app to passively collect smartphone sensor and usage data and administer daily ecological momentary assessments (EMAs) querying strength-based coping and positive affect. We will recruit 200 racially minoritized youth (ages 14-18) with diverse sexual orientations/gender identities from community health settings. Using multilevel modeling, we will test associations between intersectional identity groups and resilience outcomes. We will then apply unsupervised machine learning (e.g., k-means clustering on features like communication diversity, app usage for learning/support) to identify distinct digital phenotypes of resilience, validating them against EMA and baseline survey data on cultural strengths and community belonging.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a novel, empirically derived taxonomy of digital phenotypes of resilience specific to intersectionally minoritized youth. It will shift methodological paradigms by demonstrating how passive sensing can capture strength-based constructs, directly informing the development of preventive mental health tools that amplify existing resilience rather than solely monitoring risk.
proposals.novelty: This work inverts the standard deficit model in digital phenotyping and adolescent health disparities research by exclusively operationalizing intersectionality as a source of strength, using passive sensing to quantify resilience pathways rather than risk.

proposals.title: Capturing the Dynamic Impact: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study of Intersectional Stigma, Affective Reactivity, and Suicidal Ideation Among Racially Minoritized Youth
proposals.motivation: Structural racism and discrimination are established social determinants of mental health disparities, but their real-time, fluctuating impact on daily psychological states is poorly understood. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Williams's own work, highlights the need for fine-grained, contextual data on how intersecting stigmas (e.g., based on race, gender, sexuality) proximally influence affective states and critical outcomes like suicidal ideation in youth, beyond retrospective surveys.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that momentary exposures to intersectional stigma (e.g., racial microaggressions, LGBTQ+ discrimination) will be associated with immediate increases in negative affect and physiological arousal, and that the magnitude of this affective reactivity will mediate the association between cumulative daily stigma exposure and end-of-day reports of suicidal ideation.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 racially minoritized youth (ages 14-18) with diverse gender/sexual identities. Using a custom smartphone app (building on prior digital phenotyping work), we will implement signal-contingent Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) 5 times daily for 14 days, prompting reports of recent stigma events, current affect, and physiological stress. Event-contingent reporting will allow logging of incidents as they occur. We will use multilevel modeling to test within-person associations between stigma events and subsequent psychological states, and dynamic structural equation modeling to examine mediation pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel, high-temporal-resolution evidence on the psychological mechanisms linking lived experiences of intersectional stigma to acute mental health risk. It will advance the field beyond cross-sectional associations by modeling dynamic, within-person processes, directly informing the development of just-in-time adaptive interventions aimed at mitigating the real-time impact of discrimination.
proposals.novelty: While EMA has been used to study stress, its application to rigorously measure the immediate effects of *intersectional* stigma events in community-based youth is novel. The integration of event-contingent reporting with mediation analysis of dynamic suicidal ideation risk represents a significant methodological advance over static, between-person studies of discrimination and health.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the Normative Benchmark: A Multi-Level Analysis of Family Structure, Structural Racism, and Adolescent Mental Health Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Mental health research and clinical assessment often implicitly treat the two-parent nuclear family as the optimal or normative benchmark for healthy development. This assumption is historically and culturally specific, fails to account for the diversity of effective family structures, and pathologizes arrangements common in communities of color. Given pre-2026 evidence on the health impacts of structural racism and the lived experiences of minoritized youth, this uncritical benchmark may itself be a source of bias, obscuring strengths and contributing to health disparities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that after accounting for socioeconomic stressors and exposure to structural racism, adolescents from extended, multi-generational, or single-parent family structures will demonstrate mental health outcomes equivalent to, or in specific domains better than, those from nuclear families. Further, we posit that family cohesion and support will be a stronger predictor of mental health than family structure per se.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of longitudinal, population-representative data (e.g., Add Health, ABCD Study) using structural equation modeling (SEM) to test our multi-level model. The primary exposure will be a time-varying measure of family structure, with key moderators including a validated area-level index of structural racism and individual measures of racial discrimination. Mental health outcomes (depressive symptoms, anxiety, psychological well-being) will be modeled using mixed-effects regressions. We will employ causal mediation analysis to formally test whether family cohesion and social support mediate the relationship between structure and outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence to challenge a dominant normative assumption in developmental and clinical psychology. It will shift the focus from family form to family process and context, offering a more equitable framework for assessment and intervention. The findings will directly inform anti-racist practices in community health systems by identifying strengths in diverse family arrangements.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents associations between family structure and outcomes, this study directly inverts the nuclear family benchmark as the analytical frame. It innovatively integrates measures of structural racism as a fundamental moderator, moving beyond individual-level correlates to examine how macro-level discrimination shapes the meaning and impact of family structure on mental health.

proposals.title: The CARIBBEAN-FAM Cohort: A Multi-Country Study of Structural Racism, Family Dynamics, and Mental Health Outcomes Among Youth of African Descent
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Williams's own work, establishes that structural racism is a critical determinant of health and developmental disparities for Black youth, but this evidence is largely U.S.-centric. The Caribbean, with its unique colonial histories, diverse ethnic compositions, and varied social policies, provides a powerful natural laboratory to study how different structural contexts shape family processes and youth mental health. A harmonized, multi-country cohort is urgently needed to move beyond single-nation studies and identify both universal and context-specific pathways linking structural factors to health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that national-level indicators of structural racism (e.g., economic inequality, ethnic political representation, education policy disparities) will be associated with family-level stress and support processes, which in turn will mediate disparities in adolescent mental health outcomes (depression, anxiety, suicidality) across Caribbean nations.
proposals.method: We will establish a harmonized cohort of 5,000 adolescent-caregiver dyads across Jamaica, Trinidad and Tobago, Barbados, and The Bahamas. Data collection will integrate: 1) Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) via a refined digital phenotyping app (building on Williams's prior work) to capture real-time affect, family interactions, and suicidal ideation; 2) Cross-sectional surveys measuring perceived discrimination, family functioning, and mental health; and 3) Archival data to construct national structural racism indices (e.g., Gini coefficient, incarceration rates by ethnicity). Analysis will employ multi-level structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the hypothesized mediation pathways, accounting for nesting within countries.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first large-scale, harmonized dataset on family and youth mental health across the Caribbean, enabling direct cross-national comparisons. It will advance the field by empirically testing how macro-level structural factors 'get under the skin' via family microsystems, moving beyond U.S.-focused associations to a more global understanding of racism's health impacts. Findings will inform targeted, context-specific public health interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines structural racism and youth health within single countries, this proposal's novelty lies in its explicit multi-country comparative design within the Caribbean region, allowing for the disaggregation of universal from context-specific effects of structural racism on family and mental health outcomes.

proposals.title: Predicting Household Instability from Digital Phenotypes of Psychological Distress and Resources in Minoritized Youth: A Machine Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Research has established that structural racism and household instability are significant social determinants of youth mental health. However, the inverse relationship—whether mental health states can signal impending family structure changes—remains unexplored. This gap limits proactive, family-centered interventions, particularly for minoritized youth in community health systems who face intersecting stressors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal digital phenotyping data on psychological distress (e.g., suicidal ideation, anxiety) and protective resources (e.g., coping, social support) can predict subsequent changes in household composition (e.g., parental separation, caregiver transitions) among racially and ethnically minoritized youth.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal data from a digital phenotyping app (refined in prior work) deployed within a community health system, capturing ecological momentary assessments of affect, behavior, and resources. Household composition will be tracked via caregiver reports. We will employ a time-to-event (survival) analysis framework using Cox Proportional Hazards models with time-varying covariates to establish baseline associations. For prediction, we will develop and compare recurrent neural network (RNN) architectures, specifically Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, to model temporal sequences of distress/resource signals and forecast the probability of household change over a 3-6 month horizon.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel predictive model linking individual mental health trajectories to family-level outcomes, moving beyond cross-sectional association studies. It will provide a methodological framework for using digital phenotyping for proactive, structural social risk identification in clinical and community settings, directly informing targeted support services.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the traditional causal direction studied in social epidemiology, framing mental health data as a leading indicator rather than an outcome. It integrates digital phenotyping methods, typically used for individual risk prediction, with a focus on predicting changes in a key structural social determinant.

proposals.title: Structural Racism, Family Instability, and Cardiometabolic Risk in Adolescence: Testing Psychosocial Pathways to Hypertension
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Williams and others has established that structural racism and family instability are linked to adverse mental health outcomes in youth. However, the pathways from these social determinants to early physical health risks, particularly hypertension—a key precursor to cardiovascular disease disparities—remain underexplored. This gap is critical because cardiometabolic dysregulation often begins in adolescence, setting lifelong trajectories of inequity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to structural racism (e.g., neighborhood segregation, discrimination) is associated with increased family instability (e.g., caregiver transitions, household chaos), and that this instability mediates the relationship between structural racism and elevated blood pressure in adolescents via psychosocial pathways of chronic stress and allostatic load.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study of 800 adolescents (aged 12-16) of African descent, recruited from community health systems. We will use geocoded data to construct a multi-component structural racism index (residential segregation, poverty concentration, policing density). Family structure and instability will be measured via caregiver-reported household rosters and a validated family chaos scale. The primary outcome is ambulatory blood pressure monitoring. We will employ structural equation modeling (SEM) to test mediation, controlling for socioeconomic status, BMI, and health behaviors, using Mplus software for path analysis and bootstrapping to assess indirect effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel empirical evidence linking macro-level structural factors to a critical physical health outcome in youth through family-level mechanisms. It will extend the developmental psychopathology framework to incorporate cardiometabolic risk, offering a more integrated model of how social inequities become biologically embedded. Findings will inform targeted family-focused interventions within pediatric and community health settings to mitigate early cardiovascular risk.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links racism to mental health or uses proxy measures like self-reported health, this proposal directly tests a theoretically-grounded pathway from quantified structural racism to an objective, clinically-significant physical health biomarker (hypertension) via family processes in a youth cohort, moving beyond association to mechanistic explanation.

proposals.title: Community-Level Family Structure and Population Mental Health: A Multilevel Analysis of Structural Racism and Ecological Stress
proposals.motivation: Prior research by Williams and others establishes that structural racism shapes family formation and mental health outcomes at the individual level. However, the ecological impact of community-level family composition, as a potential manifestation of historical disinvestment and discrimination, remains underexplored. This study addresses a critical gap by examining whether the neighborhood prevalence of specific family types (e.g., single-parent, multi-generational households) functions as a community-level stressor or resource, affecting the mental health of all residents, irrespective of their own household structure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a higher community-level prevalence of family structures associated with socioeconomic adversity (e.g., single-parent households) will be associated with poorer population mental health outcomes for all neighborhood residents, and that this association will be significantly mediated by neighborhood-level indicators of structural racism (e.g., residential segregation, poverty concentration).
proposals.method: We will conduct a multilevel, cross-sectional analysis using pre-2026 U.S. data (e.g., ACS, PLACES, or county health rankings) aggregated to the census tract level. The primary exposure is the tract-level prevalence of specific family types. The outcome is a tract-level mental health indicator (e.g., average poor mental health days). We will employ spatial regression models (e.g., spatial lag or error models) to account for autocorrelation and structural equation modeling (SEM) for multilevel mediation analysis to test pathways through tract-level racial composition, economic inequality, and police-reported discrimination incidents.
proposals.contribution: This work will shift the discourse from individual risk to ecological effect, providing empirical evidence for how community family ecology, shaped by structural racism, constitutes a social determinant of mental health. It will offer a novel methodological framework for public health surveillance and inform place-based interventions that target neighborhood infrastructure and economic opportunity rather than individual family behaviors.
proposals.novelty: While individual-level associations are well-studied, this proposal innovates by conceptualizing family structure prevalence as an ecological feature and testing its population-wide mental health impact, directly integrating Williams's focus on structural racism into neighborhood-level quantitative analysis.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Community Action, Policy Advocacy, and Integrated Strategies for Advancing Youth Mental Health Equity: A Multi-Site Longitudinal Study
proposals.motivation: Structural racism is a fundamental driver of mental health inequities among minoritized youth, yet the most effective strategies for systemic change remain unclear. Pre-2026 discourse often pits community-led action against policy advocacy, creating a false dichotomy that hinders coordinated efforts. This research addresses a critical gap in evidence for practitioners and advocates seeking to deploy limited resources for maximum, sustainable impact on youth mental health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: The effectiveness and sustainability of change strategies for youth mental health equity are contingent on specific community contexts, with integrated approaches (combining community action and policy advocacy) demonstrating superior outcomes in settings with pre-existing advocacy infrastructure, while community-led action alone may be more effective in contexts of high distrust in governmental systems.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-site, longitudinal comparative effectiveness research (CER) design across 8-10 diverse community health systems. Using a convergent mixed-methods approach, we will quantitatively track youth mental health outcomes (e.g., via digital phenotyping tools for suicidal behavior) and policy adoption, while qualitatively assessing mechanisms like social cohesion and perceived self-efficacy. We will apply structural equation modeling (SEM) to test pathways and multilevel modeling to account for community-level contextual factors (e.g., political climate, historical disinvestment). A configurational analysis using Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA) will identify necessary and sufficient conditions for strategy success.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a pragmatic, evidence-based 'pathway map' detailing the optimal conditions, sequencing, and integration of bottom-up and top-down strategies. It will advance health equity science by moving beyond documenting associations between racism and health to testing actionable intervention strategies at the community-system level. The findings will provide concrete guidance for community organizations, health systems, and policymakers.
proposals.novelty: This framework moves beyond evaluating single strategies to comparatively model their synergistic interactions and contextual dependencies, a non-obvious shift from asking 'which is better' to 'what works where and why.' It uniquely integrates methods from implementation science, community-based participatory research, and political epidemiology to model dynamic change processes.

proposals.title: Developing and Piloting an Antiracist Equity Impact Assessment (AREIA) Protocol for Adolescent Health Research and Quality Improvement
proposals.motivation: Structural racism is a critical determinant of health disparities, yet scientific and QI projects often proceed without a formal, proactive analysis of their potential to perpetuate or exacerbate these inequities. Retrospective analyses, like those linking structural racism to adverse adolescent brain and health outcomes, are vital but insufficient for prevention. Drawing a direct analogy from Environmental Justice policy, this project addresses the need for a structural, preventative tool to audit research and QI designs for embedded racial bias before implementation, thereby preventing harm to minoritized youth.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the framework of Environmental Justice Impact Assessments to create a structured, community-engaged audit tool for research and QI will significantly improve the identification and mitigation of racially inequitable assumptions and potential disparate outcomes during the design phase.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase, mixed-methods study. First, a systematic review of EJIA frameworks and existing health equity tools (e.g., PROGRESS-Plus) will inform a draft protocol. Second, we will employ iterative, community-based participatory research (CBPR) methods, convening advisory boards of youth, caregivers, and community health workers from our partner community health system to co-refine the tool. The final AREIA protocol will be a structured checklist and narrative guide auditing for: representativeness in recruitment, cultural validity of measures, potential for disparate burden/benefit, and community data governance. We will pilot the tool on 3-5 proposed studies within the health system, using qualitative thematic analysis of researcher and community board feedback to assess feasibility and perceived impact.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, practical tool for institutional review boards and QI committees to mandate proactive antiracist review. It will contribute a methodological framework for translating structural racism theory into actionable, preventative audit practices, moving the field from documenting harm to preventing it. The pilot will provide initial evidence on the tool's utility for improving the equity of research and QI targeting adolescent health.
proposals.novelty: While equity checklists exist, this project innovates by directly adapting the regulatory, community-empowering structure of Environmental Justice Impact Assessments—a proven policy tool for systemic change—to the context of health science and QI. It shifts the focus from individual bias to structural audit and mandates community engagement as a core component of the scientific design approval process.

proposals.title: Operationalizing Antiracism in Community Health Systems: A Mixed-Methods Framework for Science and Quality Improvement
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, landmark advisories from the U.S. Surgeon General and other bodies have called for the explicit integration of antiracist principles into health research and systems. However, a critical gap exists in translating these broad calls into concrete, measurable, and implementable strategies within the operational workflows of community health systems, where structural racism directly impacts youth mental health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a co-designed, multi-level framework for antiracist science and quality improvement (QI), developed with community stakeholders, will be associated with measurable improvements in perceived equity of research processes and more equitable preliminary health outcomes for minoritized youth compared to standard QI approaches.
proposals.method: Using a community-based participatory research (CBPR) design, we will first conduct qualitative interviews and focus groups with youth, caregivers, and frontline staff to identify specific racist barriers in research/QI. We will then co-develop and pilot-test an antiracist framework. Quantitative evaluation will employ a quasi-experimental, stepped-wedge design across clinics, using mixed-effects models to analyze between-group differences in key process metrics (e.g., recruitment equity, intervention appropriateness ratings) and preliminary clinical outcomes (e.g., PHQ-9 scores) extracted from electronic health records and our team's existing digital phenotyping platform.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a replicable, operational framework for antiracist science and QI, directly addressing the implementation gap in current policy advisories. It will contribute empirical evidence on the association between antiracist structural interventions and both equitable research processes and improved mental health outcomes for marginalized youth in real-world community settings.
proposals.novelty: This project moves beyond documenting associations between racism and health or reviewing structural factors, to actively designing and testing a procedural intervention. It uniquely integrates antiracist praxis directly into the technical workflows of scientific research and clinical quality improvement, a systems-engineering approach not commonly applied to this problem pre-2026.

proposals.title: Community-Driven Metrics for Accountability: A CBPR Longitudinal Study on Equity Legislation Impact in Nursing
proposals.motivation: Structural racism in nursing is a critical public health issue, affecting workforce diversity, provider well-being, and patient outcomes. Pre-2026, while equity legislation (e.g., state-level mandates on implicit bias training or workforce diversity) is being proposed, its real-world effectiveness is often evaluated using top-down, researcher-defined metrics that may not capture lived experiences. This gap limits the legislation's potential for meaningful, systemic change and fails to center the voices of racialized nurses, students, and patients who are most impacted.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a longitudinal assessment framework co-designed with affected communities will identify more valid, actionable, and contextually relevant indicators of legislative effectiveness in reducing structural racism in nursing, compared to traditional expert-driven metrics, leading to a more empowered community and greater accountability.
proposals.method: This study employs a multi-phase Community-Based Participatory Research (CBPR) design. First, we will establish a Community Advisory Board (CAB) of nursing professionals, students, and patients from racialized groups to co-design the study's core metrics and data collection methods (e.g., validated surveys, narrative interviews, institutional data audits). Second, we will implement a longitudinal, mixed-methods tracking system using these metrics, collecting data at baseline and annually for three years. Data analysis will integrate quantitative methods (e.g., multi-level modeling to assess change over time, controlling for covariates) with qualitative thematic analysis, with interpretation and refinement of the framework conducted in iterative cycles with the CAB.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, community-validated metric framework for evaluating health equity policy, moving beyond association studies to a model of participatory accountability. It will generate longitudinal evidence on the specific mechanisms through which legislation does or does not impact the daily experiences of racism in nursing. The process itself aims to build community capacity for health services research and advocacy.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely fuses CBPR with longitudinal policy evaluation specifically in nursing, shifting the epistemic authority for defining 'effectiveness' from researchers to the community. It moves beyond documenting associations between racism and health outcomes to actively co-creating the tools for systemic intervention and monitoring.

ideas.title: Standardized Phenotyping Framework for Multi-National Study of Nurse Racism Experiences
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from comparative plant ecology/phytogeography to solve the scaling challenge of a multi-national comparative study on racism experiences of internationally recruited nurses. In phytogeography, scientists compare how plant species respond to environmental stressors (like drought) across different continents by using standardized 'common garden' experiments and trait-based functional frameworks to isolate the stress response from local genetic adaptation. The adapted solution is to create a 'common garden' methodological framework for the social science study. This involves developing a core, standardized set of validated instruments (phenotyping tools) to measure racism experiences, psychological impacts, and resilience factors. This identical measurement protocol would be administered to nurse cohorts in the US, Canada, and Australia. Crucially, the study would simultaneously and systematically collect data on key host-country contextual variables (the 'environment'), such as specific immigration pathways, institutional policies in healthcare workplaces, and national racial discourse metrics, to control for these factors and enable a valid comparison of the core experience of racism across nations.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Safety Climate' Model: Measuring How Systemic Racism Against Nurses Creates a 'Care Climate' Affecting Patient Outcomes
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'safety climate' model from organizational psychology to examine how racism experienced by nurses affects patient care. The analogous problem—linking systemic workplace factors to concrete negative outcomes—was solved by developing validated scales to measure the shared perceptual 'climate' and correlating it with objective performance data. The adapted idea is to create and validate a 'clinical care climate' scale that quantifies nurses' collective experiences of racism and equity in their institution. By treating this as a systemic, measurable climate variable rather than only an individual stressor, the research can then analyze this climate data against aggregated, objective patient outcome metrics (e.g., safety indicators, clinical effectiveness scores) to establish a quantifiable link between the racist environment nurses work in and the quality of care patients receive.

ideas.title: Disentangling Causal Attribution in Negative Experiences: A Systems Biology Approach to Intersectionality
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that negative experiences are solely due to racism by investigating its interplay with factors like professional hierarchy, gender, and immigration status. Drawing an analogy from systems biology's approach to complex diseases—which moved from seeking single causes to modeling multi-factorial interactions—this expanded idea adapts that solution. It proposes to develop an interaction network model for social science phenomena, mapping how racism and other identity/structure-based factors co-configure non-additively to produce specific negative outcomes. The methodology would involve identifying and quantifying synergistic or antagonistic interactions between these factors, thereby reframing the research question from 'Is it solely racism?' to 'Through what specific interactive pathways do these combined forces produce this experience?'

ideas.title: Co-designing and longitudinally tracking community-driven metrics for equity legislation impact in nursing
ideas.description: This research employs Community-Based Participatory Research (CBPR) principles to co-design both the key metrics and the methodology for a longitudinal study assessing the effectiveness of equality legislation in reducing racism in nursing. Instead of using solely researcher-defined outcomes, affected nursing professionals, students, and patients from racialized communities collaboratively establish what 'effectiveness' means and identify the most meaningful indicators of systemic change. This community-driven framework is then used to track legislative impact over time, with ongoing CBPR cycles to refine the study based on interim findings and evolving community priorities. The synergy transforms a top-down evaluation into a dynamic, community-owned tool for advocacy and accountability, ensuring the long-term study directly serves and empowers those it aims to benefit.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Justice Impact Assessments for Antiracist Science & QI Audits
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Environmental Justice, this research proposes to adapt the solution of Environmental Justice Impact Assessments to the context of antiracist science and healthcare QI. In environmental policy, EJ Impact Assessments are used to proactively identify and mitigate disproportionate burdens on marginalized communities before projects begin. This research will develop and pilot an analogous 'Antiracist Equity Impact Assessment' tool. This tool will provide a structured protocol to audit proposed research studies and QI projects for embedded racial assumptions, potential for disparate outcomes, and exclusionary practices during their design phase. By mandating this assessment and a community-engaged mitigation plan as part of the approval process, the solution institutionalizes a proactive, structural check against racism, moving beyond retrospective analysis to prevent harm at the point of conception.

ideas.description: This research investigates not which approach is 'primary,' but under what conditions different change strategies—community-led direct action, policy advocacy, or their integrated combination—are most effective and sustainable for advancing specific health equity outcomes. Using a multi-site, longitudinal comparative effectiveness design, it will measure outcomes, mechanisms, and contextual factors across diverse communities. The goal is to generate a pragmatic, evidence-based 'pathway map' for practitioners and advocates, identifying the optimal sequencing and integration of bottom-up and top-down strategies to maximize impact and systemic change.

ideas.title: Apply Context-Mechanism-Outcome configurations from realist evaluation to anti-racism interventions
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from complex public health systems evaluation, where realist evaluation's Context-Mechanism-Outcome (CMO) configuration framework was developed to understand what works, for whom, and under what conditions. The analogous solution involves building and empirically testing theories about how program resources trigger reasoning and behavioral changes (mechanisms) in participants, but only when specific contextual conditions are met, leading to observed outcomes. Adapted to anti-racism research, this means moving beyond simple pre-post measurements of interventions like training or policy changes. Instead, researchers would first articulate the program theory of an intervention: What specific components are meant to provide what resources? What participant reasoning (e.g., 'I am accountable,' 'I have new skills') is this supposed to trigger? In what organizational or societal contexts (e.g., high vs. low trust, with or without enforcement power) will these mechanisms fire? And what outcomes (attitudinal, behavioral, institutional) should result? The research would then use mixed methods (e.g., interviews, surveys, observational data) across diverse implementation sites to test and refine these CMO configurations, building a nuanced, theory-driven understanding of what anti-racism interventions work, for which groups, and in what specific contexts.

ideas.title: Applying Hostile Environment Engineering Controls to Mitigate Patient-Source Racism in Nursing
ideas.description: This research adapts the problem-solving framework of occupational health, specifically the hierarchy of controls for hazardous exposures, to the issue of racism directed at minority nurses by patients and families. The analogous field is Occupational Health and Safety, where a proven solution for protecting workers is to implement 'engineering controls'—modifying the physical environment or processes to isolate people from hazards. The adapted idea is to study and develop systemic, environmental interventions within clinical workflows that reduce direct exposure and vulnerability to patient-source racism. This shifts the focus from individual coping and reporting (analogous to PPE and administrative controls) to proactively designing care delivery systems (e.g., structured team handoffs, spatial layouts, and communication protocols) that inherently buffer nurses from racist hostility, thereby creating a safer occupational environment through structural change.

ideas.description: This research moves beyond labeling systems as racist to dissecting the specific operational logics—embedded in policies, algorithms, and standard protocols—that produce racialized outcomes. It employs a dual methodology: first, a computational audit of institutional data flows to identify statistical disparities in care pathways and resource distribution; second, targeted ethnography at the decision-points flagged by the audit to observe how structures constrain or bias professional judgment. The goal is to generate a mechanistic map of the system, pinpointing where and how interventions in policy, technology, or procedure can most effectively disrupt the production of inequity.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Tipping Point Analysis to Evaluate Equality Legislation in Nursing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecosystem ecology, where the long-term effectiveness of environmental regulations is evaluated by monitoring for systemic tipping points rather than linear progress. The analogous problem is assessing whether an intervention (like the Clean Air Act) moves an ecosystem to a new, stable healthy state. The adapted research idea applies this to nursing by conducting longitudinal studies that track a suite of quantitative and qualitative 'social health indicators' within healthcare organizations following equality legislation. The goal is to determine if the legislation acts as a catalyst for a fundamental, sustained regime shift in organizational culture and outcomes, or if its effects are incremental and reversible, thereby providing a more nuanced measure of long-term legislative impact on reducing racism.

ideas.title: Disentangling Causal Pathways: A Conjunctural Analysis of Systemic Drivers of Racial Disparities
ideas.description: This research employs a causal inference and historical sociology framework to avoid tautology and test competing explanations. It models structural racism not as a monolithic cause but as a set of measurable, historically instantiated mechanisms (e.g., zoning laws, lending practices) and estimates their causal weight relative to and in interaction with concurrent non-racialized systemic factors like class inequality or industrial change. The design uses natural experiments, counterfactual analysis, and simulation to identify which specific conjunctures of factors produce observed disparities, moving beyond a priori assumptions of primacy.

ideas.title: A Technical Framework for Bias-Correction to Improve Diagnostic Accuracy and Measurement Validity in Science and QI
ideas.description: This project establishes a methodological and computational system to identify, quantify, and correct for racial/ethnic bias embedded in the data and algorithms used for scientific discovery and quality measurement. By treating bias as a source of measurement error that threatens the validity of research findings and the fairness of performance metrics, it creates objective, replicable tools for audit and correction. This approach embeds antiracist outcomes—such as equitable risk prediction and fair quality assessments—into the core scientific processes of validation and reliability, securing buy-in based on methodological rigor rather than political alignment.

ideas.title: Network-Informed Intervention Design for Anti-Racism in Nursing
ideas.description: This research program integrates organizational network analysis (ONA) directly into the design, targeting, and evaluation of anti-racism interventions for minority nurses. By mapping formal and informal networks, it identifies not just exclusion patterns but also key influencers, communication bottlenecks, and potential allies within the system. This network intelligence is then used to design and deploy precise, context-specific interventions—such as tailored mentorship programs, bias interruption protocols, or coalition-building initiatives—through the most effective network pathways. The approach allows for the measurement of intervention impact on network structure and dynamics over time, creating a feedback loop for continuous adaptation and optimization of anti-racism strategies.

ideas.title: Intersectional Equity Auditing: Disentangling Racism from Other Power Dynamics in Organizational Systems
ideas.description: This research develops and tests a methodology for conducting intersectional equity audits of business operations (hiring, procurement, governance). It moves beyond identifying racial disparities to analytically disentangle whether negative experiences or outcomes are primarily attributable to racism, to other intersecting factors like gender or professional hierarchy, or to their synergistic interaction. The goal is to create a validated audit tool that allows organizations to diagnose the precise drivers of inequity within their systems, moving from generic anti-racism pledges to data-informed, structurally precise interventions. This models anti-racism not as a standalone initiative but as an integrated analysis of overlapping power dynamics, reforming practices based on causal attribution rather than correlation.

ideas.title: From Ward-Level Ethnography to Policy Prototyping: A Micro-to-Macro Framework for Anti-Discrimination Reform
ideas.description: This research program embeds long-term ethnographic observation within a single hospital unit to meticulously document the micro-dynamics of discrimination and structural barriers as they occur in real-time. These granular findings are not merely descriptive; they are systematically translated into draft legislation, regulatory language, and operational protocols designed to directly interrupt the observed harmful practices. The approach enables 'policy prototyping,' where the feasibility and impact of proposed changes can be modeled and refined within the ethnographic site before being scaled, ensuring advocacy is grounded in concrete, evidence-based mechanisms rather than abstract principles.

ideas.title: Testing the Moral Credentialing Hypothesis: How Manager Identity and Self-Perceptions Blindspot Recognition of Workplace Racism
ideas.description: This research program tests the specific hypothesis that managers, particularly those from historically advantaged groups or who perceive themselves as non-prejudiced, are subject to moral credentialing. This psychological mechanism leads them to systematically underestimate racism in their teams. The design employs a controlled experiment to isolate the effect of manager identity on bias detection, followed by a field study triangulating manager surveys, minority employee experience sampling, and objective audit data to map the precise conditions and magnitude of the perception gap.

ideas.title: Legislation as a Complex Intervention: Modeling Implementation Pathways to Predict Outcomes
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that Equality Acts automatically lead to progress by adapting a solution from public health intervention research. In that field, the simplistic assumption that a health program (e.g., a new drug or counseling service) will work as intended was replaced by frameworks for 'complex interventions,' which explicitly model implementation pathways and contextual factors. The analogous solution involves deconstructing an intervention into its core components, its proposed theory of change, and the necessary conditions for its success. Adapted to this context, the research would treat landmark Equality Acts as complex social interventions. The study would involve mapping each law's logical implementation pathway from passage to societal outcome, then using comparative historical or case-study analysis to identify specific points of failure—such as gaps in enforcement, counter-mobilization, or unanticipated market reactions. This shifts the research question from 'Did the law fail?' to 'Which components of the law's implementation system failed, under what conditions, and why?'

ideas.title: Institutionalizing Equity: How a Medical Association Can Leverage Positive-Deviance Models to Drive Antiracist Reform in the NHS
ideas.description: This research investigates how a national medical association, as a traditionally conservative institution, can lead antiracist transformation by systematically studying and promoting 'positive-deviance' NHS units where minority nurses report equitable integration. It moves beyond merely identifying best practices to examine the strategic, political, and operational conflicts the association faces in elevating these localized successes into a system-wide reform agenda. The study uniquely explores the tension between leveraging concrete, successful models as persuasive evidence for change and the institutional risks of endorsing radical structural critiques that challenge the status quo it traditionally upholds.

ideas.title: Matched-Pair Ethnography: Isolating Systemic vs. Contextual Drivers of Nurse Integration Equity
ideas.description: This study employs a robust comparative design to move beyond identifying positive outliers. It first uses NHS workforce data to create matched pairs of hospital units that are structurally similar but exhibit divergent outcomes in minority nurse integration and perceived opportunity. Within each pair, researchers conduct embedded observation, organizational network analysis, and in-depth interviews to map the precise mechanisms—such as decision-making structures, advocacy networks, or routine practices—that differentiate the high-integration unit from its matched counterpart. This approach isolates actionable, transferable systemic factors from unique contextual or personality-dependent factors, providing a blueprint for scalable intervention.

ideas.title: Causal Identification and Prioritization of Structural Health Interventions
ideas.description: This research system first applies rigorous causal inference techniques to historical and administrative data to empirically identify which specific structural factors (e.g., a zoning policy, a transportation funding formula) demonstrably cause quantifiable harm to population health outcomes. It then builds policy simulation models to forecast the causal impact of potential legislative or regulatory changes designed to alter those specific factors. The final output is a prioritized, evidence-based policy portfolio that ranks interventions by their predicted causal effect size, implementation feasibility, and cost-effectiveness, providing advocates and policymakers with a defensible, impact-focused roadmap for action.

ideas.title: Testing Community-Led Interventions as a 'Biological Control' for Health Equity
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that policy advocacy is the primary lever for health equity change by drawing an analogy from ecology and pest management. In that field, the top-down solution of broad-spectrum chemical pesticides (analogous to top-down policy) often causes unintended harm and resistance, leading to the adoption of 'biological control'—localized, integrated solutions that work with the existing ecosystem. Adapting this solution, the research would test if community-led, non-policy interventions (e.g., peer health networks, local asset-building) function as a form of 'biological control' for health inequities. It would compare their effectiveness and sustainability against top-down policy advocacy for specific, measurable health equity outcomes, hypothesizing that these grassroots interventions may create more targeted, resilient, and self-sustaining change within the complex 'ecosystem' of a community.

ideas.title: Unit-Level Ethnography of Policy Misinterpretation: Tracing the Micro-Dynamics of Institutional Betrayal in Nursing
ideas.description: This study combines a deep ethnographic focus on a single hospital ward with a targeted investigation of equality policy failure. It will immerse the researcher in the daily life of the unit to document not just instances of discrimination, but the specific moments where formal policies are invoked, ignored, or reinterpreted by staff at all levels to justify or obscure disadvantageous treatment of minority nurses. The synergy lies in tracing the complete 'life cycle' of a policy misinterpretation—from its genesis in a local interaction, through its normalization in ward culture, to its consequential impact on individual careers and well-being—thereby uncovering the micro-processes of institutional betrayal that macro-level policy analysis cannot capture.

ideas.title: A Realist Evaluation of Context-Mechanism-Outcome Configurations for Equitable Integration in the NHS
ideas.description: This research applies the realist evaluation framework to the inverse problem of successful integration. It investigates NHS units where minority nurses report positive outcomes to build and test program theories. The study will identify the specific contexts (e.g., leadership, team structures, policies) that trigger key mechanisms (e.g., perceived fairness, psychological safety, professional validation) to generate equitable integration outcomes. The result will be a refined, evidence-based theory specifying what configurations of interventions and contextual factors work, for which groups of minority nurses, and why, providing a actionable blueprint for replicating success elsewhere.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Microaggressions with Triggered Sampling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from behavioral neuroscience and psychophysiology to improve the experience sampling methodology for studying nurse microaggressions. In the analogous field, researchers use ambulatory physiological monitors to trigger Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) surveys when a participant shows a physiological stress or craving response, ensuring data capture is event-contingent. Adapted to this context, nurses would wear a device monitoring physiological stress indicators. When a stress signature is detected, it triggers a smartphone survey asking about the immediate social context, perceived interaction, and emotions, thereby capturing microaggression events and their acute psychophysiological context with high temporal precision and ecological validity.

ideas.title: Apply Ecological Niche Modeling to CRT Health Policy Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Ecological Niche Modeling in Conservation Biology to the context of applying Critical Race Theory to health policy analysis. In ecology, models like MaxEnt analyze environmental variables (e.g., temperature, precipitation) to predict where a species can exist, identifying critical constraints. Analogously, this research would model the 'policy environment' for cardiovascular health, treating equitable outcomes for racialized groups as the 'species' whose viability is being tested. Variables would include quantifiable and qualitative factors such as allocation of prevention funding, composition of advisory boards, historical policy legacies, and narratives used in legislative debate, all analyzed through a CRT lens. The adapted method would deconstruct racial power dynamics by systematically identifying which elements of the policy formation ecosystem most powerfully constrain equity, thereby providing a novel, evidence-based map for targeted intervention to dismantle structural barriers.

ideas.title: Mapping Managerial Blind Spots: Using Organizational Network Analysis to Discrepancy-Check Manager Perceptions of Racism
ideas.description: This research fuses organizational network analysis (ONA) with a critical examination of managerial perspectives to investigate racism in nursing. It uses ONA to objectively map patterns of social support, information flow, and mentorship, identifying structural exclusion of minority nurses. These network maps are then used as a benchmark to compare against managers' self-reported perceptions of team dynamics and equity, revealing specific discrepancies where managerial awareness fails. This approach shifts the question from 'Are managers accurate?' to 'In which specific structural network contexts are managers most likely to be blind to the exclusion experienced by their staff?'

ideas.title: Comparative Micro-Ethnography of Equitable and Inequitable Ward Cultures
ideas.description: This research conducts immersive, unit-level ethnographies in two carefully selected NHS hospital wards: one identified as a site of successful integration for minority nurses and one where discrimination is reported. By applying the same deep observational methodology to both 'positive' and 'negative' cases, the study directly compares the daily micro-interactions, routines, communication patterns, and leadership practices that constitute each ward's culture. This comparative design isolates the active ingredients of equitable integration, distinguishing them from the mechanisms of exclusion, thereby generating a fine-grained, actionable model of how inclusive clinical environments are actively built and sustained in practice.

ideas.title: Multi-modal sensor-triggered contextual sampling of workplace stress and interpersonal friction
ideas.description: This study employs a passive, multi-sensor array (audio, proximity, and physiological wearables) to continuously capture objective data on nurse mobility, social interactions, and stress arousal during shifts. An algorithm detects anomalous patterns in this data stream, such as co-occurring elevated heart rate variability and specific vocal stress signatures. These detected anomalies then trigger a brief, context-aware ESM prompt on the nurse's device, asking about their immediate subjective experience and perception of the preceding interaction. This method triangulates objective behavioral markers with subjective reports, capturing events that may not be consciously identified as 'microaggressions' while minimizing reactivity and recall bias inherent in purely self-initiated diary methods.

ideas.title: Dynamic Network-Embedded Experience Sampling for Mapping Microaggression Propagation
ideas.description: This study integrates real-time experience sampling methodology (ESM) with longitudinal organizational network analysis (ONA) to investigate the social ecology of microaggressions against minority nurses. By prompting nurses to report microaggressions via a mobile app, the ESM captures the immediate event, perpetrator, and emotional impact, while the concurrently mapped ONA data identifies the perpetrator's position in advice, trust, and social networks. This fusion allows researchers to model how network centrality, clique membership, and hierarchical power differentials predict both the likelihood of committing microaggressions and the subsequent spread of exclusionary norms through the network, moving beyond static correlation to dynamic, structural causation.

ideas.title: Institutional Capture or Accountable Transformation? Designing External Covenants for Antiracist Change in Medical Associations
ideas.description: This research investigates how traditionally conservative medical associations can be bound to genuine antiracist transformation through formal, externally enforced accountability structures. It moves beyond examining internal leadership conflicts to analyze real-world cases where associations have entered into legally or morally binding 'accountability covenants' with independent racial justice coalitions. The study will compare the design, enforcement mechanisms, and outcomes of such covenants to identify which features—such as shared governance, public progress metrics, and community veto power—most effectively prevent institutional capture and co-optation. The goal is to provide a replicable model for structural change that transfers substantive power and oversight outside the association's traditional hierarchy.
